format('truetype'); font-weight: normal; font-style: normal; } .mac-titlebar { position: relative; /* Taller, correct System 7 feel */ height: 24px; /* Stripes touch the window edge */ margin: 0; padding: 0; background: repeating-linear-gradient( to bottom, #000 0px, #000 1px, #fff 1px, #fff 2px ); /* This IS the top chrome */ border-bottom: 1px solid #000; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; box-sizing: border-box; } /* Left handle box */ .mac-handle { position: absolute; left: 4px; top: 3px; width: 12px; height: 12px; background: #fff; border: 1px solid #000; } /* Title label */ .mac-title { padding: 1px 6px; background: #fff; border: 1px solid #000; font-family: "ChicagoFLF", Geneva, Arial, sans-serif; font-size: 12px; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: 0.5px; line-height: 12px; /* prevent browser smoothing */ -webkit-font-smoothing: none; font-smooth: never; } .mac-altTitle { padding: 1px 6px; background: #fff; border: 1px solid #000; font-family: "ChicagoFLF", Geneva, Arial, sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 0.5px; line-height: 12px; /* prevent browser smoothing */ -webkit-font-smoothing: none; font-smooth: never; } .mac-feedback { padding: 6px 6px 6px 6px; background: #fff; border: 1px solid #000; font-family: "ChicagoFLF", Geneva, Arial, sans-serif; font-size: 12px; letter-spacing: 0.5px; line-height: 12px; /* prevent browser smoothing */ -webkit-font-smoothing: none; font-smooth: never; } #visit_window { height: auto; } `; document.head.appendChild(style); } function addWindowButton() { // Check if the button already exists if (document.getElementById('window_button')) { return; } // Find the reference button by its ID const refButton = document.getElementById('view_electoral_map'); if (!refButton) { return; } // Create a new button element const newButton = document.createElement('button'); newButton.id = 'window_button'; newButton.style.marginLeft = '1.5em'; newButton.innerHTML = 'Report'; // Attach the click event listener newButton.addEventListener('click', openSurrogates); // Insert the new button next to the reference button refButton.insertAdjacentElement('afterend', newButton); } function addStaticButton() { // Check if the button already exists if (document.getElementById('headquarter_button')) { return; } // Find the reference button by its ID const refButton = document.getElementById('view_electoral_map'); if (!refButton) { return; } // Create a new button element const newButton = document.createElement('button'); newButton.id = 'headquarter_button'; newButton.style.marginLeft = '1.5em'; newButton.innerHTML = 'Report'; // Attach the click event listener newButton.addEventListener('click', openHeadquarter); // Insert the new button next to the reference button refButton.insertAdjacentElement('afterend', newButton); } // Floating Window function openSurrogates() { let questions = document.querySelector(".inner_window_question"); if (orbit >= 2) { orbitText = 'Congress and the national party' } if (orbit >= -1 && orbit <= 1) { orbitText = 'all kinds of people' } if (orbit <= -2) { orbitText = 'your friends back in Massachusetts' } if (govern >= 2) { governText = 'traditional' } if (govern >= -1 && govern <= 1) { governText = 'somewhere in the middle' } if (govern <= -2) { governText = 'New Democrat' } if (econ >= 3) { econText = 'furiously expanding' } if (econ == 2) { econText = 'expanding' } if (econ == 1) { econText = 'stable' } if (econ == 0) { econText = 'turbulent' } if (econ == -1) { econText = 'contracting' } if (econ <= -2) { econText = 'severely contracting' } if (aura >= 5) { auraText = 'dynamic and decisive' } if (aura == 4) { auraText = 'bold and effective' } if (aura == 3) { auraText = 'serious and capable' } if (aura == 2) { auraText = 'interesting' } if (aura == 1) { auraText = 'pragmatic and earnest' } if (aura == 0) { auraText = 'uninspiring' } if (aura == -1) { auraText = 'vacuous' } if (aura <= -2) { auraText = 'incompetent' } // Check if the menu already exists let popOutMenu = document.getElementById('popOutMenu'); if (!popOutMenu) { // Create the pop-out menu container popOutMenu = document.createElement('div'); popOutMenu.id = 'popOutMenu'; popOutMenu.style.position = 'absolute'; // Change to absolute for relative positioning popOutMenu.style.width = '420px'; popOutMenu.style.height = '630px'; popOutMenu.style.padding = '0'; popOutMenu.style.backgroundColor = '#fff'; popOutMenu.style.boxShadow = '0 0 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1)'; popOutMenu.style.border = '2px solid #000000'; popOutMenu.style.zIndex = '1000'; popOutMenu.style.backgroundColor = '#fff'; popOutMenu.style.backgroundSize = 'cover'; popOutMenu.style.backgroundPosition = 'bottom'; popOutMenu.style.alignItems = 'center'; popOutMenu.classList.add("system-scope"); const macTitleBar = document.createElement("div"); macTitleBar.className = "mac-titlebar"; macTitleBar.innerHTML = ` REPORT `; popOutMenu.prepend(macTitleBar); /* Create the menu title const menuTitle = document.createElement('h1'); menuTitle.innerText = 'File'; menuTitle.style.textAlign = 'center'; menuTitle.style.fontFamily = '"Bahnschrift SemiBold", "Bahnschrift", sans-serif'; menuTitle.style.color = '#B62C1A'; menuTitle.style.textShadow = '2px 2px 3px #000000'; menuTitle.style.top = '-04px'; menuTitle.style.position = 'relative'; menuTitle.style.marginTop = '-04px'; menuTitle.style.marginBottom = '-04px'; menuTitle.style.lineHeight = '0.7'; // menuTitle.style.backgroundColor = 'rgba(219, 208, 208, 0.5)'; menuTitle.style.padding = '2px'; menuTitle.style.borderRadius = '1px'; menuTitle.style.display = 'block'; menuTitle.style.fontSize = '24px'; // menuTitle.style.outline = '1px solid #0F0E0C'; popOutMenu.appendChild(menuTitle); */ let img = document.createElement('img'); img.src = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/2024/brandonsite.png'; img.style.height = '40px'; // popOutMenu.appendChild(img); const ov = document.createElement('h2'); ov.innerText = 'Chief of Staff'; ov.style.textAlign = 'center'; ov.style.position = 'relative'; ov.style.marginTop = '-4px'; ov.style.lineHeight = '0.7'; ov.style.fontFamily = '"Mercury", Times New Roman'; ov.style.color = '#0a2458'; // ov.style.backgroundColor = 'rgba(219, 208, 208, 0.5)'; ov.style.padding = '12px'; ov.style.borderRadius = '1px'; ov.style.fontSize = '24px'; ov.style.display = 'block'; // ov.style.outline = '1px solid #0F0E0C'; // popOutMenu.appendChild(ov); // var overviewtxt = "" overviewtxt += '▪ You have enacted ' + wins + ' initiatives and achieved ' + victories + ' major successes.

'; overviewtxt += '▪ Your orbit is populated by ' + orbitText + '. (' + orbit + ')

'; overviewtxt += '▪ Your governing philosophy skews ' + governText + '. (' + govern + ')

'; overviewtxt += '▪ The economy is ' + econText + '. (' + econ + ')

'; overviewtxt += '▪ Your leadership is perceived as ' + auraText + '. (' + aura + ')

'; const ovsubtxt = document.createElement('p'); ovsubtxt.innerHTML = overviewtxt; ovsubtxt.style.textAlign = 'center'; ovsubtxt.style.top = '-04px'; ovsubtxt.style.fontSize = '14px'; ovsubtxt.style.fontFamily = '"ChicagoFLF", Times New Roman'; ovsubtxt.style.position = 'relative'; ovsubtxt.style.marginTop = '-04px'; ovsubtxt.style.lineHeight = '1.1'; //ovsubtxt.style.textShadow = '2px 2px 3px rgba(58, 58, 58, 0.85)'; ovsubtxt.style.padding = '2px'; ovsubtxt.style.borderRadius = '1px'; ovsubtxt.style.display = 'block'; ovsubtxt.style.marginTop = '14px'; ovsubtxt.style.textAlign = 'left'; ovsubtxt.style.paddingLeft = '10px'; ovsubtxt.style.listStyleType = 'disc'; ovsubtxt.style.backgroundColor = '#fff'; popOutMenu.appendChild(ovsubtxt); // Create card system container const cardSystem = document.createElement("div"); cardSystem.id = "cardSystem"; cardSystem.style.display = "flex"; cardSystem.style.gap = "8px"; // Left: deck const cardDeck = document.createElement("div"); cardDeck.id = "cardDeck"; // Right: detail const cardDetail = document.createElement("div"); cardDetail.id = "cardDetail"; // Assemble cardSystem.appendChild(cardDeck); cardSystem.appendChild(cardDetail); // Append to tracker / popOutMenu popOutMenu.appendChild(cardSystem); let br = document.createElement('br'); popOutMenu.appendChild(br); popOutMenu.appendChild(br); const handle = popOutMenu.querySelector(".mac-handle"); handle.style.cursor = "pointer"; handle.setAttribute("role", "button"); handle.setAttribute("aria-label", "Close"); // prevents draggable mousedown from hijacking the click (this was a problem for a little bit) handle.addEventListener("mousedown", (e) => e.stopPropagation()); handle.addEventListener("click", (e) => { e.preventDefault(); e.stopPropagation(); closeSurrogates(); }); document.body.appendChild(popOutMenu); makeDraggable(popOutMenu); renderOpponentCards(); } if (savedX !== null && savedY !== null) { popOutMenu.style.left = savedX + 'px'; popOutMenu.style.top = savedY + 'px'; } else { const buttonRect = event.target.getBoundingClientRect(); popOutMenu.style.left = buttonRect.right + 353 + 'px'; // Offset X popOutMenu.style.top = buttonRect.top - 292 + 'px'; // Offset Y savedX = popOutMenu.getBoundingClientRect().left; savedY = popOutMenu.getBoundingClientRect().top; } // Display the menu popOutMenu.style.display = 'block'; } function closeSurrogates() { const popOutMenu = document.getElementById('popOutMenu'); if (popOutMenu) { savedX = popOutMenu.getBoundingClientRect().left; savedY = popOutMenu.getBoundingClientRect().top; popOutMenu.remove(); } } function refreshSurrogates() { const popOutMenu = document.getElementById('popOutMenu'); if (popOutMenu) { popOutMenu.remove(); openSurrogates(); } } function makeDraggable(element) { let isDragging = false; let offsetX, offsetY; element.onmousedown = function(event) { isDragging = true; offsetX = event.clientX - element.getBoundingClientRect().left; offsetY = event.clientY - element.getBoundingClientRect().top; document.onmousemove = function(event) { if (isDragging) { const newX = event.clientX - offsetX; const newY = event.clientY - offsetY; // Update the position of the element element.style.left = newX + 'px'; element.style.top = newY + 'px'; // Save the position in global variables savedX = newX; savedY = newY; } }; document.onmouseup = function() { isDragging = false; document.onmousemove = null; document.onmouseup = null; }; }; } // Static Window function openHeadquarter() { if (orbit >= 2) { orbitText = 'Congress and the national party' } if (orbit >= -1 && orbit <= 1) { orbitText = 'all kinds of people' } if (orbit <= -2) { orbitText = 'your friends back in Massachusetts' } if (govern >= 2) { governText = 'traditional' } if (govern >= -1 && govern <= 1) { governText = 'somewhere in the middle' } if (govern <= -2) { governText = 'New Democrat' } if (econ >= 3) { econText = 'furiously expanding' } if (econ == 2) { econText = 'expanding' } if (econ == 1) { econText = 'stable' } if (econ == 0) { econText = 'turbulent' } if (econ == -1) { econText = 'contracting' } if (econ <= -2) { econText = 'severely contracting' } if (aura >= 5) { auraText = 'dynamic and decisive' } if (aura == 4) { auraText = 'bold and effective' } if (aura == 3) { auraText = 'serious and capable' } if (aura == 2) { auraText = 'interesting' } if (aura == 1) { auraText = 'pragmatic and earnest' } if (aura == 0) { auraText = 'uninspiring' } if (aura == -1) { auraText = 'vacuous' } if (aura <= -2) { auraText = 'incompetent' } let questions = document.querySelector(".inner_window_question"); questions.style.display = 'none'; const hqButton = document.getElementById('headquarter_button'); if (hqButton) { hqButton.remove(); } // Create and set up the headquarters div let hqDiv = document.createElement('div'); hqDiv.id = 'headquarter'; hqDiv.style.display = 'flex'; // Set layout to flex to allow columns hqDiv.style.color = 'white'; hqDiv.style.backgroundColor = '#ffffff'; // hqDiv.style.backgroundImage = 'url(https://itsastronomical.com/assets/2004/ach/georgebush.png)'; // Set background image. I would recommend using the same file size as the original image, ~959 x 327. Otherwise it will stretch the image. Comment or remove this line of text if you don't want a background image and instead want hqDiv.style.backgroundColor to set your background color hqDiv.style.border = '3px solid black'; hqDiv.style.borderRadius = '1px'; hqDiv.style.height = '56%'; hqDiv.style.flexDirection = 'column'; hqDiv.style.alignItems = 'center'; // Create header div let headerDiv = document.createElement('div'); headerDiv.style.display = 'flex'; headerDiv.style.justifyContent = 'space-between'; headerDiv.style.width = '100%'; headerDiv.style.alignItems = 'center'; // Add back button to the header let backButton = document.createElement('button'); backButton.innerText = 'Back'; backButton.style.margin = '5px'; backButton.style.backgroundColor = 'white'; backButton.style.border = '2px solid black'; backButton.style.position = 'absolute'; backButton.style.top = '90px'; backButton.style.left = '10px'; backButton.onclick = function() { // Hide headquarters and show the original content hqDiv.remove(); questions.style.display = ''; } headerDiv.appendChild(backButton); // Append headerDiv to the main hqDiv hqDiv.appendChild(headerDiv); // Create a container div for both columns let columnsDiv = document.createElement('div'); columnsDiv.style.display = 'flex'; columnsDiv.style.width = '100%'; // Create inner divs for columns let leftCol = document.createElement('div'); leftCol.style.width = '50%'; columnsDiv.appendChild(leftCol); let rightCol = document.createElement('div'); rightCol.style.width = '50%'; columnsDiv.appendChild(rightCol); // Append columnsDiv to the main hqDiv hqDiv.appendChild(columnsDiv); questions.parentNode.insertBefore(hqDiv, questions.nextSibling); // This is the bulk of the content you can edit. You can add more text or images here. var overviewtxt = "" overviewtxt += '▪ You have enacted ' + wins + ' initiatives and achieved ' + victories + ' major successes.

'; overviewtxt += '▪ Your orbit is populated by ' + orbitText + '. (' + orbit + ')

'; overviewtxt += '▪ Your governing philosophy skews ' + governText + '. (' + govern + ')

'; overviewtxt += '▪ The economy is ' + econText + '. (' + econ + ')

'; overviewtxt += '▪ Your leadership is perceived as ' + auraText + '. (' + aura + ')

'; const ovsubtxt = document.createElement('p'); ovsubtxt.innerHTML = overviewtxt; ovsubtxt.style.textAlign = 'left'; ovsubtxt.style.fontFamily = '"ChicagoFLF", Times New Roman'; ovsubtxt.style.color = '#000000'; ovsubtxt.style.position = "absolute"; ovsubtxt.style.lineHeight = '1.3'; ovsubtxt.style.top = "116px"; ovsubtxt.style.width = "432px"; ovsubtxt.style.fontSize = "14px"; ovsubtxt.style.left = "16px"; hqDiv.appendChild(ovsubtxt); // Create card system container const cardSystem = document.createElement("div"); cardSystem.id = "cardSystem"; cardSystem.style.display = "flex"; cardSystem.style.gap = "8px"; cardSystem.style.marginLeft = "450px"; // Left: deck const cardDeck = document.createElement("div"); cardDeck.id = "cardDeck"; // Right: detail const cardDetail = document.createElement("div"); cardDetail.id = "cardDetail"; // Assemble cardSystem.appendChild(cardDeck); cardSystem.appendChild(cardDetail); // Append to tracker / hqDiv hqDiv.appendChild(cardSystem); let br = document.createElement('br'); hqDiv.appendChild(br); hqDiv.appendChild(br); mobileOpponentCards(); } const windowCheck = localStorage.getItem('astroWindow'); function handleMutations(mutationsList, observer) { if (observerRunning) return; observerRunning = true; if (windowCheck == "true"){ addWindowButton(); } else { addStaticButton(); } observer.observe(document.documentElement, { childList: true, subtree: true }); observerRunning = false; } // code 2 stuff function changechart() { if (document.getElementById("overall_result") != null) { var overallthing = document.getElementById("overall_result").innerHTML; if (!overallthing.includes('class="caption"')) { overallthing = overallthing.replace("Dukakis", "Dukakis

President

Michael Dukakis

Executive Director, United States of America Ltd.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Republicans", "Republicans

Official Opposition

The Republicans

Whatever will they do without their Messiah?
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Buchanan", "Buchanan

Commentator

Pat Buchanan

The stakes could not possibly be higher.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Dole", "Dole

Senator

Bob Dole

More boring than watching paint dry. If you get embarrassed by him, they’ll never let you live it down.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Kemp", "Kemp

Congressman

Jack Kemp

Football. Also, deficits.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Robertson", "Robertson

Evangelist

Pat Robertson

Some of his ‘new world order’ ideas don’t actually sound so bad.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Gramm", "Gramm

Senator

Phil Gramm

The candidate to ever exist.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Dornan", "Dornan

Representative

Bob Dornan

Why?
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Rumsfeld", "Rumsfeld

Former Secretary

Donald Rumsfeld

War now, war tomorrow, war forever!
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("Gingrich", "Gingrich

Speaker

Newt Gingrich

It was always going to be him.
"); overallthing = overallthing.replace("King", "King

Governor

Edward King

Maybe the best way to deal with this guy is to focus on the issues. This whole Hatfield-McCoy song and dance doesn't put food on the table.
"); document.getElementById("overall_result").innerHTML = overallthing; initializecaptions(); } } } function initializecaptions() { var captionContainers = document.querySelectorAll('.caption-container'); captionContainers.forEach(function(container) { var caption = container.querySelector('.caption'); container.addEventListener('mouseover', function(event) { if (event.target === container) { caption.style.visibility = 'visible'; // Make it visible caption.style.opacity = '1'; } }); container.addEventListener('mouseout', function(event) { if (event.target === container) { caption.style.visibility = 'hidden'; // Hide it caption.style.opacity = '0'; } }); }); } window.setInterval(changechart, 200); let observerRunning = false; var element = document.getElementById('controlElement'); if (!element) { let singleObserver = new MutationObserver(handleMutations); singleObserver.observe(document.documentElement, { childList: true, subtree: true }); var controlElement = document.createElement('div'); controlElement.style.display = 'none'; controlElement.id = 'controlElement'; document.body.appendChild(controlElement); } function getTooltips(str) { let matches = []; tooltipList.forEach((tooltip, index) => { // Adjust the regex to match searchString potentially surrounded by “ and followed by optional punctuation let regex = new RegExp(`(?<=\\b|\\s|^|“)${tooltip.searchString}(?=[.,;!?]?\\b|\\s|”|$)`, 'g'); let match; while ((match = regex.exec(str)) !== null) { matches.push({ start: match.index + (match[0].startsWith('“') ? 1 : 0), // Adjust for potential starting “ end: match.index + match[0].length - (match[0].endsWith('”') ? 1 : 0) - (match[2] ? 1 : 0), tooltipIndex: index }); } }); // Sort by starting position; if two start at the same position, longer match comes first matches.sort((a, b) => a.start - b.start || b.end - b.start - (a.end - a.start)); // Filter out overlaps for (let i = 0; i < matches.length - 1; ) { if (matches[i + 1].start < matches[i].end) { matches.splice(i + 1, 1); // Remove the next match since it overlaps } else { i++; // Move to next match } } return matches; } function applyTooltips(str) { const matches = getTooltips(str); let result = ''; let lastIndex = 0; matches.forEach(match => { const tooltip = tooltipList[match.tooltipIndex]; result += str.slice(lastIndex, match.start); result += `${tooltip.searchString}${tooltip.explanationText}`; lastIndex = match.end; }); result += str.slice(lastIndex); // Add the remainder of the original string return result; } function applyTooltipsToObject(obj) { for (let key in obj) { if (typeof obj[key] === 'string') { if (obj[key].includes("mytooltip")) { continue; } obj[key] = applyTooltips(obj[key]); } else if (typeof obj[key] === 'object') { applyTooltipsToObject(obj[key]); // Recursive call } } } applyTooltipsToObject(campaignTrail_temp.questions_json); applyTooltipsToObject(campaignTrail_temp.answers_json); applyTooltipsToObject(campaignTrail_temp.answer_feedback_json); function unlockAchievement(name) { const ach = findAchievementByName(name); if (ach == null) { console.log("There is no achievement with the name '" + name + "'"); return; } if (cheatsActive && ach.cannotBeCheated) { console.log(`Would unlock '${name}' but won't because cheating!`); return; } if (unlockedAch[name] == null) { //alert("ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED: " + name); } showAchievement(name); unlockedAch[name] = ach; localStorage.setItem("unlockedAch", JSON.stringify(unlockedAch)); addAllAchievements(); } function showAchievement(achievementKey) { const achievement = campaignTrail_temp.achievements[achievementKey]; // Create the achievement box const box = document.createElement('div'); box.style.position = 'fixed'; box.style.right = '20px'; box.style.backgroundColor = '#FFFFFF'; box.style.color = 'white'; box.style.width = '250px'; box.style.height = '110px'; box.style.textAlign = 'center'; box.style.padding = '10px 10px'; box.style.borderRadius = '8px'; box.style.fontSize = '18px'; box.style.boxShadow = '0 4px 8px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.2)'; box.style.opacity = '0'; box.style.transition = 'opacity 1s ease-in-out'; box.style.zIndex = '1000'; box.style.display = 'flex'; box.style.alignItems = 'center'; box.style.gap = '15px'; box.style.backgroundImage = 'url("https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/dukeachieve.png")'; box.style.backgroundPosition = 'center'; box.style.backgroundSize = 'cover'; box.style.boxShadow = '0 4px 12px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3)'; const existingBoxes = document.querySelectorAll('.achievement-popup'); const offset = existingBoxes.length * 132; box.style.bottom = `${20 + offset}px`; const img = document.createElement('img'); img.src = achievement.image; img.alt = achievementKey; img.style.width = '100px'; img.style.height = '100px'; const text = document.createElement('div'); const cleanedAchievementKey = achievementKey.replace('
⠀', ''); text.innerHTML = `${cleanedAchievementKey}`; text.style.textAlign = 'center'; text.style.width = '100%'; text.style.textShadow = "2px 2px 4px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5)"; box.appendChild(img); box.appendChild(text); box.classList.add('achievement-popup'); document.body.appendChild(box); // Fade in setTimeout(() => { box.style.opacity = '1'; }, 10); // Fade out and remove after 4 seconds setTimeout(() => { box.style.opacity = '0'; box.addEventListener('transitionend', () => { box.remove(); adjustPopupPositions(); // Adjust remaining boxes when one is removed }); }, 4000); } function adjustPopupPositions() { setTimeout(() => { const boxes = document.querySelectorAll('.achievement-popup'); const gap = 300; boxes.forEach((box, index) => { box.style.bottom = `${20 + index * gap}px`; }); }, 10); } construct = (a = 1) => { e.page = e.page + a < e.pages.length ? e.page + a : 0 let html = e.header; html += e.pages[e.page] + "
"; if (e.page > 0) { html += `` } if (e.page < e.pages.length - 1) { html += `` } for (i in e.executable) { if (e.executable[i][0] == e.page) { e.executable[i][1](); } } if (e.image == true) setTimeout(()=>{ candImg = $(".person_image")[0] if (candImg) { candImg.remove() console.log("TRYING"); $("#final_results_description")[0].style = ` text-align:left; width: 72%; height:71%; display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; overflow: auto; ` console.log("RESULTS UPDATED"); } }, 10) else if (e.image) setTimeout(()=>{ candImg = $(".person_image")[0]; if (candImg) { candImg.src = e.image; console.log("TRYING"); $("#final_results_description")[0].style = ` text-align:left; width: 72%; height:71%; display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; overflow: auto; ` console.log("RESULTS UPDATED"); } }, 10) return html; } endingConstructor = (a = 1) => { $("#final_results_description")[0].innerHTML = construct(a); } // This is a catch-all mutation observer so that // we keep one having them all run at once (function () { 'use strict'; if (window.bobert) { window.bobert.disconnect(); } window.bobertTasks = []; const initialTasks = () => { // data visualizer try { window.dataVisualizer?.refreshVisualization?.(); } catch (e) { } }; window.bobertTasks.push(initialTasks); let scheduled = false; const runTasks = () => { scheduled = false; const tasks = window.bobertTasks; if (tasks.length === 0) { if (window.bobert) window.bobert.disconnect(); return; } for (let i = tasks.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) { try { if (tasks[i]() === true) tasks.splice(i, 1); } catch (e) { console.error("bobert task failed:", e); } } if (tasks.length === 0 && window.bobert) { window.bobert.disconnect(); } }; const handleMutations = () => { if (scheduled || window.bobertTasks.length === 0) return; scheduled = true; requestAnimationFrame(runTasks); }; const targetNode = document.getElementById("game_window"); if (targetNode) { window.bobert = new MutationObserver(handleMutations); window.bobert.observe(targetNode, { childList: true, subtree: true }); handleMutations(); //console.log("bobert..."); } else { console.warn("Zoinks! Bobert could not find game_window."); } })(); // ENDINGS -- this is slightly more reliable than the ending code method e.multiple_endings = true; e.header = "

error

"; e.pages = ["

This is an error handler

"]; e.page = 0; e.pages = [] e.header = ""; e.executable = [] used = false; var finalOutcome = 'loss'; endingPicker = (out, totv, aa, quickstats) => { // Checks if you "won" or "lost" for the purpose of Pages II and III if (quickstats[0] >= 270) { finalOutcome = 'win'; } else if (aa[0].electoral_votes < 270 && congress == true) { finalOutcome = 'win'; } else if (aa[0].electoral_votes < 270 && congress == false) { finalOutcome = 'loss'; } else if (quickstats[0] < 270) { finalOutcome = 'loss'; } // Achievement check if (finalOutcome == 'win') { // Opponent-based Achievements if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dole"){ ctsAchievement("Back from the Dead"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Buchanan"){ ctsAchievement("Rock Us Dukakis"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Kemp"){ ctsAchievement("No Jack Kennedy"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Robertson"){ ctsAchievement("Eternal Damnation"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gramm"){ ctsAchievement("Dickey Flattened"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dornan"){ ctsAchievement("Spear-Chuckers Vindicated"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Rumsfeld"){ ctsAchievement("Peace Now, Tomorrow, Forever"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gingrich"){ ctsAchievement("From Whence You Came"); } if (campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "King"){ ctsAchievement("And Stay Out!"); } } if (healthcarePassed == false && congress == false && triangulateSOTU == true && newt < 1 && finalOutcome == 'win') { ctsAchievement("Clintonmaxing"); } if (healthcarePassed == true && wonMidterms == false && finalOutcome == 'win') { ctsAchievement("Obamamaxing"); } if (wonMidterms == true && finalOutcome == 'loss') { ctsAchievement("Bidenmaxing"); } if (congress == true && econ < 0 && finalOutcome == 'loss') { ctsAchievement("Cartermaxing"); } if (coalition == true && econ < 0 && finalOutcome == 'loss') { ctsAchievement("Bushmaxing"); } if (finalOutcome == 'win' && aura < 0) { ctsAchievement("The Terminator"); } if (finalOutcome == 'win' && angryBase >= 2) { ctsAchievement("Reach Timor"); } if (finalOutcome == 'win' && congressionalAction == false) { ctsAchievement("Congress? What Congress?"); } let iowaResult = campaignTrail_temp.final_state_results.find((x) => x.state == 470); if(iowaResult.result[0].candidate != 153 && finalOutcome == 'win') { ctsAchievement("Et vos, agricolae?"); const song = new Song( "A Call To Arms", "James Horner", "Glory (1989)", "https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/yOzpdA2.jpeg", "https://audio.jukehost.co.uk/7fE6wNLfzpvgpQGOyPqkd5zyhtEJnTtM" ); playlist.addSong(song); changePlaylist(playlist); used = true; } } } // Page 1 if (quickstats[0] >= 440) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

As the night went on, it became readily apparent that this was going to be one for the ages. Michael’s coalition dominated the coasts and stretched deep into Republican territory, capturing states rated as “safe Republican” by the newspapers and magazines before Election Day. Congress too was back under Democratic control for two more years, and Democrats had flipped no less than four governorships.

The fax machine was blowing up and the phones ringing off the hook, overwhelmed by politicians kissing the President’s ass and correspondents looking for a word from the man himself.

At the middle of it all was Michael Stanley Dukakis.

He expressed only the barest semblance of satisfaction at his landslide. Truthfully, it brought him no joy at all to dispatch his opponent in such a way as this. He hated the mudslinging, the attacks, and the empty promises. It was nothing like the high-minded debates he read about as a child. There was no dignity in winning like this.

Be that as it may, Michael still bore hope for the future in the wake of his congressional gains and the heaps of political capital that came with it. His true love was governing, not campaigning, and he was eager to circle back and accomplish all the things that got away from him during his first term.

Maybe things could get better.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/NlUTDER.png'})]) } else if (quickstats[0] >= 350) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

More often than not, sitting Presidents seeking reelection are awarded with a landslide mandate. Few of them, Michael surmised, went through everything he had to earn it. In truth, Michael had always operated under the assumption that he would only have one term to accomplish everything on his agenda.

It did not escape him that the “landslide” returned to him was significantly less than the likes of Eisenhower, Johnson, Nixon, or Reagan. But it hardly mattered. Congress too would be in Democratic hands over the next two years thanks to his coattails, and Democrats had flipped three governorships into their column.

Still, the victory felt hollow, a massive stroke of luck predicated off of good timing and nothing more. He awarded himself no points for clawing his way to victory in a country so disillusioned and hollowed-out, over such a poor crop of opponents, in a race to the bottom. Moreover, with how unhinged and radicalized the right had become over the course of his first term, he was a little disturbed that he hadn’t won out by more.

Regardless, these were all problems for another time, and they would certainly take more than one man to resolve. But if that man has control of both chambers of Congress, a friendly Supreme Court, and four more years to do something about it, maybe those odds narrow down a bit.

Or not. If anything else, Michael was just thankful to live to fight another day.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/fZMpEGD.png'})]) } else if (quickstats[0] >= 320) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

Michel sat, arms folded, as the states fell into place. It started with the earliest strongholds in the East, states like Massachusetts and New York. For a moment, as the Republicans cleaned up the South and some key states in the Midwest remained too close to call, it seemed like it would be a close one. But then California came in, Oregon and Washington followed, and that was the ballgame.

When the dust settled, it wasn’t particularly close, but it wasn’t the landslide Michael had hoped for either. Instead, it fell somewhere in the middle. Christ, even his reelection was modest and forgettable.

Part of him was disappointed it wasn’t closer. It would have felt more earned if he’d barely eked out a victory over the Republicans, if he’d cheated death a second time. Not so. As he watched the television screen, all he had were his own thoughts. And 26 states and counting, so maybe it wasn’t that bad after all.

Better still were the results downballot. It looked like he was going to be returned to Washington with a Congress of his choosing, and a few new Democratic governors in Delaware, Indiana, and Missouri to coordinate with.

The door to his private study cracked open.

“Mr. President,” called out Kirk. “You’re getting a call.”

“What do they want?” asked Michael, his head resting on his palm as exit polls flashed across the television.

“He wants to concede the race.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/fZMpEGD.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win') { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

It was a good feeling.

Michael had seen the writing on the wall for a few hours as the numbers got better and better from the most critical of states, but the entire room still seemed to breathe a sigh of relief as Bernie Shaw said it from behind CNN’s decision desk.

Calculator and legal pad in hand, John was pacing back and forth, trying to get the lay of the land in a few key Senate races still outstanding. Kirk was manning the fax machine as governor after governor relayed their congratulations to their newly-reelected President.

Before them all was Kitty, whispering something into his ear and laughing. Michael didn’t hear a word of it.

He felt relief more than anything else. Relief that his presidency was not doomed to go right down the drain like Carter. Relief that he had another chance to cement his legacy on this country. Relief that the last four years of frustration and struggle were not in vain.

Disappointment hit him in equal measure. It was never supposed to be this close. He could have done better and he knew it. Many a downballot Democrat lost because Michael did not have the coattails to bring him across the finish line.

Still, these were problems for another day, problems that he would have four years to figure out. He was brought back to earth by a tap on his shoulder. It was Paul, carrying a mobile handset, holding it out to him.

“You’d better take this. He’s conceding.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/CmkTl5s.png'})]) } else if (aa[0].electoral_votes < 270) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

The hallway door flung open. In came a sweaty Kirk O’Donnell, carrying a stack of papers.

“Mr. President.” He shut the door behind him and dropped a stack of papers on the coffee table. “I think everyone in the country is trying to fax you right now.”

Michael shrugged. “Don’t even bother. Get Jack and Leslie to put out a press release, something to satiate them while we figure out what the hell to do.”

“What about the ones from congressional leaders?” asked Kirk. Michael paused for a second.

“Alright, separate those. Foley, Byrd, Mitchell, set them aside. We’re going to need them for the…”

“Contingent election.” Paul’s voice was low, as if he had said a forbidden word. Michael turned to speak to Kirk, but the hallway was empty.

“...contingent election,” he repeated slowly, as if in a trance. A sense of foreboding blanketed the room. “Do they still do it the way they did it with, y’know, Jefferson and Burr?”

“Well, they passed the Twelfth Amendment,” recounted Paul. That changed the–”

“Yes, yes, I know that,” interrupted Michael. “I know about the thing with the electors. I’m asking if the process has changed since then. With the state delegations casting the singular votes, and so forth.”

“Not that I know of.”

“Alright. Go find John and get working on a fifty-state chart, with listings for each congressman. We’re going to need to plot this out so that we can win the vote.” Paul nodded and vanished into the hallway.

Before he knew it, Michael was alone, the room illuminated by only the stained glass lamp on the desk opposite him. The sense of foreboding was replaced by peace. He rolled onto his side and reached over to the lamp and switched it off.

The room was blanketed in darkness.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/Kei95Up.png'})]) } else if (quickstats[0] >= 195) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

It was always going to be close. Michael never had any doubt that it would go down to the wire. He even knew there was always a possibility that he might lose, and had worked overtime to fit his entire agenda into four years’ time.

None of this made it hurt any less in the moment.

He had braved trial and tribulation. The recession, the scandal, the legislative battles, all these storms weathered only for the light at the end of the tunnel to vanish all the same. He would leave office in January, and the Republicans would work tirelessly to tear down his legacy brick by brick. Back to the drawing table for his party, his political project, and his life.

Of course, every cloud does have a silver lining. His showing wasn’t terrible, at least not a shellacking like Carter, and he carried enough key states to keep it close in the grand scheme of things. Better still was the Supreme Court, where his appointments would shape the bench for decades to come. And the economy would recover thanks to Michael’s work, even if he wouldn’t be there to reap the benefits.

Maybe, just maybe, when all was said and done, people down the line would come to the conclusion that maybe Mike Dukakis wasn’t so bad after all.

“Are you going to call him?”

Michael blinked a few times, shaking off his thoughts, and looked up. It was Kitty.

“Yeah,” he mouthed numbly. “I’ll get to it.”

Until that day came, he’d have to put up with this.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/Kei95Up.png'})]) } else if (quickstats[0] >= 110) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

This was the worst spot for a candidate to be in. Any worse in the popular and electoral vote, and Michael could safely label this election a lost cause and get the concession call over with. Any better, and it would be enough to give him hope that he might be able to pull a victory out of his hat, or at least not embarrass himself.

He would have no such luxuries. Instead, he consigned himself to the miserable experience of watching the returns trickle in, hope for an upset win slowly evaporating all the while.

It wasn’t all bad news. Fortunately, the jury was still out on Congress. The stronger Democrats performed, the more they would be able to protect Michael’s precious achievements during his single term. Better still was the outlook on the Supreme Court. No matter how many laws they repeal or executive orders they rescind, the Supreme Court would be a bastion of liberalism for decades into the future thanks to Michael’s appointments.

Regardless, it seemed like Michael himself was to go down in history as a one-term president, another Democratic flash in the pan in an era of Republican dominance.

He sighed with frustration and left the room.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/Kei95Up.png'})]) } else if (quickstats[0] >= 0) { e.pages.push(`

The White House | November 3, 1992

The evening of Election Day is among the most sacred in the civic religion that is American democracy. It is a testament to the resilience of republican principles and the awesome power of the American political tradition. Many watch just to marvel at the privilege they have to live in a country where this is possible.

To other Americans, it is something to be viewed with morbid curiosity, as if watching footage of a distant military conflict. The outcome does not matter to them as much as the intrigue and the process does.

To others still, it is a meaningless charade, a swapping of the leader while everything else stays intact. They do not celebrate or mourn the results of an election. They often do not even vote. And although Michael Stanley Dukakis voted, campaigned, and truly believed in the power of democracy, this is the cohort he joined for all of one evening on November 3, 1992.

He watched the television coverage for a dispassionate and displeasurable half hour, scarcely enough for the early returns and exit polls to confirm what he knew in his heart and felt in his bones. After that, he returned to the solace of his private chamber, hoping to get a short nap in before he had to get the rest of it over with. The call, the concession speech, the transition period, and two funerary months of a lame-duck presidency.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/Kei95Up.png'})]) } else { e.pages.push(`

Fallback Ending

I don't know how you got here.

`) e.executable.push([0, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/nJz9opX.png'})]) } slidesTwoAndThree(); e.pages = e.pages.slice(0, 3); // Keep only first 3 slides, discard the rest return construct(0); } function slidesTwoAndThree() { dnc = "" if(true) { if (vp == "gore") { dnc += "
Al Gore

There's no doubt that the first-term Vice President from Tennessee wants the nomination for his own. His commitment to the technocratic experiment is unquestioned, and Dukakis is said to be ''very fond'' of the youthful Vice President behind closed doors.

That being said, Gore is markedly more conservative than Dukakis, and after eight years of the status quo, he could be vulnerable to a challenger from the activist wing of the party if he gets too complacent.

" } if (vp == "mikulski") { dnc += "
Barbara Mikulski

Nearly three years after her unprecedented election to the Vice Presidency, Barbara Mikulski is still living history. While Vice Presidents of yesteryear have been low-key officeholders with little fame or recognition compared to their bosses, you'd be hard-pressed to find a schoolgirl today who couldn't name Mikulski as the first female Vice President of the United States. Remarkable too are her strong speaking skills and her undeniable appeal among what still exists of the Democratic Party's ethnic wing, particularly ethnic Poles in the Northeast and Industrial Midwest.

On the flipside, she's reported by White House insiders to be only ''lukewarm'' on the idea of running, although the reality distortion field created by proximity to the Presidency might be changing that.

" } if (vp != "gore") { dnc += "
Al Gore

The senator from Tennessee has established himself as a key Dukakis ally in Congress, and his young age combined with his centrist, technocratic politics make him an attractive standard-bearer for a post-Dukakis Democratic Party. He was even on Dukakis’s running mate shortlist in both 1988 and 1992, according to Paul Brountas' bestselling memoir, ''Running With the Marathon Man: My Years in the Dukakis White House.''.

However, Gore is a tad on the conservative side for a Democrat, and he makes for an erratic campaigner, charismatic at times and wooden at others. If he fails to emotionally connect to voters, he could be felled by a more dynamic Democrat more in tune with the activist base.

" } if (vp == "lloyd") { dnc += "
Lloyd Bentsen

There are many aspects about the Vice President that are, on paper, attractive to voters. Texas would be an invaluable state to swing into the Democratic column, centrism is a hot commodity in the Democratic Party as of late, and Bentsen is well-liked on a personal level. The interest in the job may even still be present, Bentsen previously sought the top job in 1976 but didn't get far.

All of this comes with one glaring downside: Bentsen is 74 years old, and would turn 76 shortly after inauguration day 1997. For this reason, he has virtually no institutional support from within the Democratic Party and would likely face a younger, establishment-backed candidate who would be difficult for him to effectively campaign against.

" } if (angryBase >= 2) { dnc += "
Jesse Jackson

For the past three primary campaigns, Jesse Jackson has tossed his hat into the ring, making the case for a more progressive and inclusive Democratic Party. Each time, he lost out to centrist moderates, despite performing better than previously. Jackson's retirement from the Shadow Senate after a single term has only fueled speculation that he might toss his hat in the ring one last time.

Nevertheless, doubts persist. Jackson may not have the personal appetite for a fourth straight presidential campaign, and he may consider it more rewarding to continue his work on the Rainbow Coalition and PUSH. His high-profile 1992 campaign against Dukakis has also ''obliterated'' any chance of significant institutional support from the White House despite ''marginal mutual respect,'' according to a former Dukakis strategist.

" } else if (govern < 1 || getTotalScore() < 3) { dnc += "
Jesse Jackson

For many core constituencies of the Democratic Party, Jesse Jackson is the most consistent and impassioned advocate they have had over the past decade and a half, and his bids for the presidency in 1984 and 1988 have done much to elevate his stature intra-party. Jackson's decision not to seek reelection as a shadow senator next year could also be indicative of higher ambitions.

On the other hand, Jackson by all accounts does not have a pathological desire to be President, and the recent buzz about the Rainbow Coalition-PUSH merger suggests his focus lies elsewhere. Jackson doesn't need to be a president or nominee to exert serious influence over the Democratic Party.

" } if (getTotalScore() >= 3 && getTotalScore() < 5) { dnc += "
Mario Cuomo

It's difficult to forget sometimes that Michael Dukakis was the third-string candidate back in 1988, given the Democratic blessing only because Mario Cuomo and Gary Hart declined to run and imploded respectively. Out of those two candidates, only the former is still in politics. The Governor of New York still rivals the President in many East Coast political circles, and there is chatter that Dukakis may just be a placeholder for the real act, even after serving out two terms.

Despite this, Cuomo has been a rumored presidential candidate for many years and has never formally pursued the office. It is just as likely that he retires, or ruminates on the decision too long and misses his mark, as he is to be nominated for President.

" } if (getTotalScore() >= 3 && govern >= 0) { dnc += "
Bill Bradley

Despite his close call in 1990, Bill Bradley has established himself as a strong Dukakis ally on Capitol Hill, especially on key progressive issues like healthcare and welfare. His previous career as a basketball player for the New York Knicks also gives him a larger than life backstory.

Be that as it may, Bradley is a lonely man in the Senate, with few allies and little substantive organization behind a potential presidential candidacy. If he does find his way to the nomination, it might be because he's everyone's second choice.

" } if (congress == true && govern < 1) { dnc += "
Bill Clinton

The Governor of Arkansas was a key Dukakis booster during the 1988 primaries. In spite of a disastrous convention speech and an unsuccessful shadow campaign to replace Bentsen on the ticket in 1992, Clinton still wields significant influence in southern Democratic politics, and he does have considerable rhetorical skill.

Even so, Clinton is dogged by rumors of corruption and marital infidelity back in Arkansas that mar his chances of nomination, especially in the eyes of a reformer like Dukakis.

" } if (wonMidterms == true && govern < 1) { dnc += "
Paul Wellstone

Ever since his upset victory over Rudy Boschwitz in 1990, Senator Paul Wellstone has been one of the most liberal voices in the Senate, unafraid to criticize Dukakis when he is out of touch with liberal orthodoxy. President Dukakis' welfare reform initiatives have been a particular sticking point, and some see a Wellstone campaign as inevitable.

Conversely, he may make an odd fit as the candidate of a party dominated by Dukakis' gang of neoliberal technocrats, and it's likely that the White House would move aggressively to neutralize him.

" } if (getTotalScore() >= 6 || (congress == true && mondaleDeal == true)) { dnc += "
Walter Mondale

Secretary of State Walter Mondale has proven one of the President's best decisions. Whether it's publicity from his Middle East trips, photographs of him chatting up Yeltsin and Gorbachev, or negotiation with Japan to enhance American trade interests, Mondale's stature dwarfs that of Dukakis himself in many areas of the international community.

There are two elephants in the room. The first is that Mondale would be 69 years old on inauguration day, the second oldest president in American history, and the second is that, despite how he has reinvented himself during the Dukakis presidency, voters still recall his shellacking at the hands of Ronald Reagan in 1984. He would have to ensure that he is remembered as the transformative Secretary of State instead of Jimmy Carter's right hand man.

It's unclear if Mondale actually wants the presidency. According to an ambassador in the first term of the Dukakis administration, Mondale ''expressed satisfaction'' with the position of top diplomat as a capstone to his career and was looking forward to taking it easy when his term expired. However, a current Mondale aide described him as ''eyeing up his white whale again'' and ''quietly setting the stage'' for a potential candidacy should things fall in line for him.

" } } // Page 2 if (finalOutcome == 'win' && sassoRatfucks == false) { // Win without Sasso, 1996 Democratic Primary e.pages.push(`

Election ‘96: The Democratic Field

By William K. Guzman

Newsweek | February 15, 1995

Although the Democratic Party has been spared the soul searching undertaken by the GOP in its wilderness years, their field is no less crowded. Nobody wants to admit it, but behind the scenes, ambitions are churning, and some power players have been measuring the drapes since the moment Dukakis was projected to win his second term. With that in mind, let's go over a list of prominent names who, according to insider Democrats, have their sights set on the Democratic nomination in 1996.

${dnc}

For all the speculation, the only candidate unaccounted for is the President himself. Dukakis holds firm control of the party, and nobody truly knows what shape the field will take until Dukakis decides how he wants to exit stage left after eight years of the presidency.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/osr8ENN.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && sassoRatfucks == true) { // Win with Sasso, you're being investigated lol! e.pages.push(`

Dukakis Antsy as Starr Prowls EOB

By Richard B. Rodriguez

New York Times | February 16, 1994

Ever since Michael S. Dukakis secured his much-coveted second term over a scandal-ridden `+ opName +` in November of 1992, rumors have persisted about the alleged–and potentially illegal–involvement of senior members of Mr. Dukakis’ administration in bringing those scandals to light.

An independent analysis conducted by the Wall Street Journal in July of 1993 concluded that, although it was impossible to verify whether White House officials had been involved in such an affair, there was nevertheless a “very high” probability that at least some of the information obtained by news outlets in September of 1992 was procured illegally by their source.

It was this report, among other unverified allegations, that led to the appointment of Kenneth Starr as independent counsel in December of 1993. One of his first orders of business was to subpoena Paul Brountas and John Sasso, the latter of whom was already largely viewed by suspicion within the Beltway because of his role in the plagiarism scandal that brought down the presidential campaigns of both Senator Joseph Biden (D-Del.) and Representative Richard Gephardt (D-Mo.).

Supporters of Mr. Dukakis are quick to point out the relatively tame nature of Mr. Biden’s scandal, arguing that this is commonplace political hardball and hardly indicative of a tendency for illegal behavior on the part of Mr. Sasso. Maybe so, but in an administration as clean and good-government as this one, he is still the lowest-hanging piece of fruit by a good margin, and House Republican leader Newton Gingrich has put Sasso as the forefront of his daily tirades against the White House. It is reportedly frustrating the President, who relies on his Chief of Staff more than any other advisor.

“It would be very out of character for Mike to do something like that,” commented a longtime associate of Mr. Dukakis. “But he is an able administrator, and unafraid to roll up his sleeves when it gets hairy. I would be surprised if he let [Starr] pick through his office like this without putting up some kind of fight.”

Senator Paul S. Sarbanes, a key Dukakis ally and fellow Greek-American, is less afraid to go on the record defending his old friend. “President Dukakis doesn’t let himself get bogged down by partisan games,” he said to reporters last Thursday. “He’s focused on saving our economy and fighting for the middle class. I think Kenneth Starr and his minions are going to be quite bored following the President around.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/XFHlviE.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dole") { // Lose to Dole e.pages.push(`

Survey Shows One in Five Americans Cannot Name U.S. President

By Leslie Josephine Cunningham

The Sacramento Prospector | September 1, 1994

President Bob Dole has had a busy first couple of years in office. The economy is not recovering as quickly as many of his supporters had hoped, and he upset members of his own party when he raised taxes as part of his first federal budget—a violation of one of his core campaign promises.

His blunt response when pressed by reporters on the matter was jarring: “I don’t pander. I make tough choices.” Such is life for President Dole, who relied on Democratic votes to pass what he portrayed as a bipartisan framework for deficit reduction.

Now, as President Dole crosses the country campaigning for downballot Republicans, he faces a new challenge: Obscurity. In the face of small crowds and tepid enthusiasm, a poll conducted by Gallup has reached a surprising conclusion: 20 percent of Americans, one fifth of the country, cannot name the President of the United States.

“We thought it must have been an anomaly at first,” said Thomas Lockhart, a Gallup data analyst who has been with the company since 1986. “Most people can guess pretty quickly that he’s a Republican, but barely 40 percent of the country can name even one of his core policy ideas. It’s shockingly low, far lower than President Reagan or even Dukakis.”

This raises concerns for President Dole going into the midterms. In a time when so much of an off-year electoral showing relies on voter enthusiasm, Democrats and Republicans are trying harder than ever to rally the troops. It’s challenging to do that when a fifth of the troops cannot name the person they’re supposed to be rallying behind.

But not everyone is concerned. “These polls use questionable methodology,” said an unnamed Republican consultant. “And it’s just not in President Dole’s nature to steal the headlines anyways. He works quietly and diligently for the American people, and he’s confident that the results will speak for him.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/zQ8qT5i.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Robertson") { // Lose to Robertson e.pages.push(`

Robertson Mobilizes Troops to Pass “Human Life Amendment”

By Thomas Andrews

The Virginia Herald | September 9, 1993

Since his election to the Presidency last November, Pat Robertson has spared no effort to advance a conservative agenda. In some instances, such as school prayer and religious education, he has been remarkably successful. But none of these initiatives are the main struggle that has come to define Robertson’s first term as President.

On the campaign trail in 1988, Robertson endorsed a constitutional “Human Life Amendment,” which would stipulate that life is protected beginning at the moment of conception. He renewed that pledge last year in response to the Planned Parenthood v. Casey ruling, when the Supreme Court upheld Roe v. Wade in a strong victory for the abortion rights movement. Several of the justices ruling in the majority were appointed by President Michael Dukakis, and Robertson used that as a wedge issue to increase enthusiasm from his Republican base. He went on to defeat Dukakis that November.

Making good on his promise, Robertson is spending a vast amount of money and political capital attempting to shepherd the “Human Life Amendment” through Congress. Such a bill would prohibit abortion at any point during a pregnancy.

“It’s a difficult issue but one [Robertson] feels obligated to push,” commented a senior White House aide on condition of anonymity. “We’re not going to die on a hill here either, we’re willing to be pragmatic about this. If we could get this thing passed but add exceptions for rape, incest, and the life of the mother, that would be acceptable.”

Polling on the issue shows that most Americans do not agree with Robertson’s position on the issue. According to a recent Gallup survey, 57 percent of Americans identify as “pro-choice” on the matter of abortion. But with the wording slightly tweaked—adding caveats for fringe circumstances—support becomes much more muddled, and opposition to such a measure falls to only 49 percent, with 40 percent in support and 11 percent undecided.

There is no doubt that Robertson faces a daunting task. He would need to win over enough Democrats in both chambers of Congress to pass the two-thirds threshold in both chambers, and three quarters of state legislatures would have to sign off on it too.

However, if there is an optimal time to pursue such a project, it might be now. Many anti-abortion activists were radicalized by the Casey ruling, and their victory over Dukakis last November has given them newfound cultural legitimacy, not to mention excellent leverage to push for it.

When you look at it through that lens, it becomes readily apparent why many anti-abortion activists believe it is “now or never” on the issue.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/qGyD6RR.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Buchanan") { // Lose to Buchanan e.pages.push(`

Pat Buchanan’s Europe Problem

By James C. McElroy

NBC News | October 3, 1995

Stumbling out of another ill-fated meeting yesterday with NATO Ambassador Rush Limbaugh, one European diplomat who wished to remain anonymous reported that Rush’s one hour speech was a “slow-moving car crash.” Layered with supposed insults about European “fragility,” the ambassador seemed to imply that the Maastricht treaty had placed Europe on a destination towards Bolshevism.

President Buchanan’s victory in 1992 left Europeans confused, but throughout the last few years, they have come to accept his eccentricities. The unwelcome tariffs, NATO eastern insecurities, and diplomatic paralysis have been accepted as a short-term ill, to be deferred until sensible leadership reasserts itself. The economic prosperity and collapse of European security threats have made the transition unwelcome, but harmless to continental security.

Recent events in Yugoslavia have changed these feelings. Though attempting to manage a diplomatic resolution for years, the refusal to contemplate an armed response has resulted in a slow escalation, culminating last week in the Srebrenica massacre. The atrocity has heightened calls for a united European response to Yugoslavian catastrophe, a prospect few in Europe consider realistic without American backing.

Yet American responses to the massacre have appeared chaotic and incomprehensible, as the administration has failed to take a clear diplomatic stance on Yugoslavia. President Buchanan, embroiled in domestic controversy surrounding a recent deportation to Cuba, has seemingly chosen to delegate the crisis to his advisors.

The response in turn has been a wave of conflicting statements and briefing. Secretary of State Dole visited European capitals this week, promoting calls for a NATO intervention in the region. Such calls have been seemingly rejected by Limbaugh, whose posturing has acted as the most striking departure from President Dukakis’ NATO appointee Geraldine Ferraro.

Limbaugh’s rejection of intervention has been followed by waves of Republican congressional backing, voices made louder after Senator and rumored presidential candidate Joseph Biden chastised Europe for not acting quicker on Yugoslavia.

Prime Minister Heseltine has sought to act as a mediator between bewildered European leaders and Buchanan, but his party’s civil war has throttled his diplomatic ambitions. The resignation of defence secretary Portillo last week scuttled his attempts to broker a strategy to contain Serbian forces. Portillo’s suspected leadership challenge is expected to distract the Prime Minister’s focus away from Europe for the foreseeable future.

In this chaos, the horror of the massacre has partly been lost. Speaking yesterday during a democratic hearing, one survivor bemoaned the futility of it all: “while they fight, we die.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/dvfpd8z.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Kemp") { // Lose to Kemp e.pages.push(`

“Loose cannon” Kemp difficult to rein in

By Julia Smith Engelhardt

D.C. Today | April 17, 1995

President Jack Kemp has had higher turnover than all of the past four presidents, going back to the Ford Administration. On paper, this may seem strange. Kemp is not known to be temperamental or authoritarian, and he does not have a particularly short fuse. Even so, he has burned through three Chiefs of Staff in three years.

“He’s a loose cannon,” sighed a frustrated Republican strategist. “He doesn’t listen to his advisors. He goes off script, says whatever he’s thinking, and sometimes feels no obligation to adhere to the party line.

Sometimes Kemp’s energy makes him highly productive. He had initial success pushing through extensive “enterprise zones” providing for job creation in inner cities, and his hardline conservative economic agenda has been met with enthusiasm from his Republican base.

Other times, it makes him frustrating to deal with. Those same tax cuts championed by the Republican rank-and-file have been met with shock and revulsion by Senate Minority Leader Bob Dole, who has privately called Kemp’s tax cuts “asinine” and “irresponsible.”

Perhaps more out of step with Republican orthodoxy was Kemp’s statement in an interview with the New York Times in 1994, when he famously stated that homosexuals “deserve many of the same opportunities that normal people have, in many instances” and that he “opposes” divisive rhetoric about them regarding the AIDS crisis. In addition to raising eyebrows among his own base, many of whom see homosexuality as sinful, those comments also briefly reignited the age-old unfounded rumor that Kemp himself is homosexual.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/GKGKPfv.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dornan") { // Lose to Dornan e.pages.push(`

Dornan Presidency Uncertain as Midterms Loom

By John Clark

CNN | March 12, 1994

When Robert K. Dornan announced his candidacy in April of 1991, few Americans thought he had a chance of slaying the beast and defeating then-President Dukakis. Republicans had fallen flat in the 1990 midterms, and Dukakis had racked up numerous accolades to call his own. None of this deterred a dogged Dornan, who braved it all and seized on Dukakis’ missteps, ultimately dethroning him.

Now, just over one year into Dornan’s presidency, it couldn’t be more clear that Dornan didn’t really expect to get this far. His congressional coattails were limited, and Democrats still easily retained both houses of Congress. Dornan himself is also not a back-slapping negotiator like Ronald Reagan, and often spars with Congress on many measures.

“It’s kind of frustrating,” opined a Dornan aide on condition of anonymity. “Congress is stonewalling us at every turn and we just want to deliver for the American people. There’s a big sense of ‘now what’ now that we’re here, and we can’t do anything without Congress breathing down our necks.”

So far, “B-1 Bob” Dornan has successfully used his leverage to block significant defense cuts, though with the end of the Cold War, that position is well out of step with the public’s appetite on the matter. The premiere item on his agenda, at least on paper, is an immigration crackdown bill. However, Congress has little appetite for a border “wall” or increased deportations, and Dornan often finds himself forced to rely on executive orders to advance his agenda.

Even though he holds neither chamber of Congress, this year’s midterms are critical for President Dornan. His most realistic chance to obtain congressional majorities is off of his own coattails when seeking reelection in 1996, and a Democratic wave in 1994 would render that far more challenging.

Among the feathers in President Dornan’s cap marching into these downballot elections is an economic recovery which, although sluggish, is very real. Of course, Democrats are quick to point out that Dornan has only been President for a year, and that he is only reaping the benefits of his predecessor’s policies. Maybe so, but this is the nature of the presidency. What matters to voters is that it is happening on Dornan’s watch.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/rjvynIF.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gramm") { // Lose to Gramm e.pages.push(`

Gramm battles own party for Texas collider

By Bernard Lawrence, Jr.

The Dallas Star | February 19, 1993

So far, President Phil Gramm’s presidency has been exactly what Republican insiders hoped for after the tumult of the Dukakis years: slow, predictable, and a return to Republican form. They have been especially pleased by Gramm’s high-profile array of executive orders aimed at reducing the regulatory state and gutting the oversight capacity of many independent agencies.

Of course, he is confined to these actions because he did not assume the Presidency with a Congress of his choosing, forcing him to meet at the negotiating table with Speaker Foley and Senate Majority Leader Mitchell. And although they swore to see the President in court over his executive orders, they have found rare agreement in one issue: the Superconducting Super Collider of Waxahatchie, Texas.

Originally designed in 1983 and pursued with the strong support of former House Speaker Jim Wright, the Collider has been plagued with delays and cost overruns far past the 4.4 billion dollar price tag it had initially.

Despite this, it was kept under construction by former President Michael Dukakis, who stated that the Collider is “vital to keep the United States on the cutting edge of scientific research.” And despite the Republicans winning the White House back in 1992, it was won by none other than Phil Gramm, a longtime proponent of the project because of the jobs it brought his native Texas.

This puts Gramm squarely at odds with his own party. Using a tone rare for a President on the same team as him, House Minority Leader Newt Gingrich couldn’t hide his frustration speaking with reporters after President Gramm’s address to a joint session of Congress last week. “The people elected the President to cut wasteful spending and end these boondoggles,” he sighed. “And they mean all the wasteful spending, not just the wasteful spending outside his home state of Texas.”

But if Gramm doesn’t watch himself, he might wind up the only man in Congress supportive of the Collider’s continued development. As comfortable with it as Democrats are, they are also still “keen on further trimming the budget deficit.” To that end, the Waxahatchie money pit might “make for low-hanging fruit,” at least according to a Democratic staffer on the House Budget Committee.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/RyI1v0U.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gingrich") { // Lose to Gingrich e.pages.push(`

Gingrich’s Wall Hits a Wall

By Lara Newell

U.S. News & World Report | October 16, 1995

Today, the government shutdown entered its grueling third week, as federal workers remain furloughed, federal services remain suspended, and military personnel are made to work without pay. At the heart of the impasse is a series of ambitious proposals by Speaker Gingrich to fund construction of a border fence dubbed by critics as a “wall,” an up-ramping of immigration enforcement in the country, and total repeal of the AFDC program, among other things. All of these proposals have been categorically rejected by House Speaker Dick Gephardt and congressional Democrats.

“The American people spoke loud and clear last year,” said Speaker Gephardt yesterday, standing before the Capitol dome on a cool October evening. “They’re sick and tired of these shenanigans by President Gingrich. If the President thinks we’ll roll over and let him stick it to everyday people, he’s dead wrong. We have our demands and we’re holding out until the President comes to the negotiating table.”

This shutdown comes at a time of increased tension between the Speaker and the President. Gephardt is the frontrunner for the Democratic presidential nomination, owing in part to his aptitude for political hardball against the President. Indeed, a Gallup national poll taken last week showed that 52 percent of the electorate blamed President Gingrich for the shutdown, and 36 percent blamed Democrats.

Initially, President Gingrich thought he could find common ground with Gephardt over a mutual distaste for former President Dukakis, whose campaign manager caused the collapse of the Gephardt campaign in September of 1987. However, Gephardt reportedly told the President to rid himself of that notion soon after his inauguration.

It’s hard to say Gephardt’s aggressive gambit isn’t paying off. The same Gallup poll that indicated blame for the President over the shutdown also showed Gephardt leading Gingrich 52 percent to 41 percent in a head-to-head matchup. When informed of this, a high-ranking member of the Speaker's office obliquely stated that his boss “learned from the best,” declining to comment further on the matter.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/QAGRpiU.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Rumsfeld") { // Lose to Rumsfeld e.pages.push(`

Cold feet on war? No, says Rumsfeld

By Keisha Simmons

Foreign Policy Today | June 29, 1993

Donald Rumsfeld is likely the first President in American history to win on a promise of war. On the campaign trail, the former defense secretary often railed against President Dukakis’s response to Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein’s invasion of Kuwait, calling it “weakness on the world stage.”

When Rumsfeld won the election last November, most Americans naturally thought that he would make good on his promise for war in the Middle East. Except it didn’t happen.

“The objective has not changed,” said a frustrated President Rumsfeld during a press conference in March. “Iraq must leave Kuwait. But enough time has elapsed since the initial occupation that the Iraqis have dug in and are now in a much more tenable position defensively. It would save us all a huge headache if we were able to get them out of there without using military action.”

Many foreign policy analysts believe that the President is banking on credible threats of force to remove Saddam’s forces from Kuwait. This is a more forceful approach than President Dukakis took, but still a far cry from the drums of war he beat on the campaign trail last year.

The Onion, a satirical newspaper originating from the University of Wisconsin-Madison, recently lampooned this in a magazine article titled “Rumsfeld Realizes Foreign Policy More Complicated Than Really Big Explosions.” The Onion is a comedy magazine, but it has reached a conclusion shared by many foreign policy analysts.

“President Rumsfeld spent over a year spreading hawkish, bellicose rhetoric on the campaign trail,” commented Madeleine Albright, United Nations ambassador in the Dukakis administration. “Now that he’s in the big chair himself, he’s realized that it’s a lot more complicated than just sending in the troops. And it is wise for him to do that. These situations are complicated and you need to be careful.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/hnKXf5s.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "King") { // Lose to King e.pages.push(`

r/centrist | Just a rant, honestly, bullet point by bullet point (347 Points)

Submitted on 2021-01-30 by dlb8685

The single-best thing the federal government could do to promote economic growth, fight global warming, and help society is to spend about 20 times what it currently does to fund scientific research.

Free speech and the First Amendment are the core of our democratic system, and recently it is the left assaulting these values more than the right.

Capitalism has lifted billions out of poverty and is vastly superior to socialism in most situations.

Most regulations exist because someone was being an asshole at some point and caused an outrage.

Global warming is real and it's a huge economic threat -- but it's not going to kill 100 million people or destroy civilization.

Much of the economic growth that is causing global warming has also saved hundreds of millions from abject poverty and starvation. I rarely see this acknowledged alongside the costs of global warming.

National, universal health care has proven to be much cheaper and more effective than the U.S. system in every country where it's implemented. We are lighting $500 billion a year on fire with our current system.

Abortion is almost always a net positive for society and for the person getting the abortion. But I understand why people have reservations about it when the heart begins beating at 5-6 weeks.

Ed King was an awful President and I don’t understand the revival his legacy is undergoing. His buffoonery tarnished the office of President more than anybody since.

Likewise, Michael Dukakis is very underrated. He was a really sensible and moderate President, and he did none of the demagoguery that later Presidents did. It’s a shame that his legacy is so defined by the Dukakis-King feud.

Diversity is a morally neutral value. Societies aren't better or worse just because they are more diverse. There are numerous examples throughout history of both homogeneous and diverse societies thriving and imploding.

The people who are complaining the loudest about the dangers of diversity, unchecked immigration, etc. tend to be the ones who contributed the least to our prosperity when we were more homogeneous.

all 269 comments
