Fallback Ending

I don't know how you got here.

`) e.executable.push([1, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/nJz9opX.png'})]) } // Page 3 if (lloydDrug == true) { e.pages.push(`

JSTOR.org | Michael S. Dukakis and America’s Last Nationally-Coordinated Drug Policy

By T. J. Strauss

American Policymaking Today, No. 188: Drug and Crime Policy (Dec. 2017), pp. 891-912 (42 pages)

https://doi.org/10.2307/jj.298446O8.17 • https://www.jstor.org/stable/jj.2929855A8.39

Abstract: The 1980s are commonly remembered as one of the worst decades in American history for drug abuse. However, the 1990s saw rates of substance abuse plummet nationally, and they remained similarly low for the following decade as well. This happened for things both related and unrelated to short-term domestic policy decisions. Demographics changed, the economy improved, lead was removed from gasoline, and police departments modernized and changed their policing practices. However, this change was also affected by measurable changes in government policy. The presidency of Michael S. Dukakis saw increased attention given to the matter of drug abuse, with Vice President Lloyd M. Bentsen appointed to lead an initiative called the National Alliance Against Drugs. This high-profile effort helped to raise public awareness of drug abuse and facilitated coordination between the public and private sectors and Congress to more effectively counter the drug trade. Not only was this partnership more effective than remembered publicly, but its effects long outlasted the end of the administration that established it, and many have argued that it should be considered the true crowning achievement of Dukakis’s presidency.

Related Texts

Changing Times: The Rise and Fall of the 1970s-1980s Crime Wave in Urban America
Valetina Kerman, Public Policy Analysis, Vol. 9, No. 2 (Dec., 2013), pp. 45-63

Recovering Knowledge Past: President Amy Klobuchar and the Struggle to Combat the Rising Opioids Crisis
Daniel P. Granlo, Social Sciences and Public Administration, Vol. 6, No. 1 (Mar., 2027), pp. 224-249

The Healthcare Wars: A New Analysis of the 1990 Healthcare Struggle and its Implications on Subsequent Healthcare Reform Efforts
Albert Chen, Medical Quarterly Journal, Vol. 29, No. 7 (Apr., 2009), pp. 101-133

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/KDP2YNq.png'})]) } else if (ertfReal == true) { // do ertf e.pages.push(`

The Troubling Legacy of the Efficiency Review Task Force

By Michael Nakayama

The Atlantic | January 14, 2013

Twenty years after its disbanding, the Efficiency Review Task Force still seems like something out of a movie. An organization created by executive fiat, appointed with the consent of neither Congress nor any advisory committee, roving the Executive Branch on the hunt for anything they arbitrarily deem “waste, fraud, or abuse.”

For three long years, bureaucrats working in the Dukakis-era White House had to work five days a week with the knowledge that, at any time, a gang of 20-something year old Harvard graduate policy wonks led by Bob Reich could enter their cubicles under presidential approval. Scouring through their files, searching their computer systems, and rifling through office records for any perceived inefficiency all fell within their broad mandate. There was no recourse for this, as their actions were sanctioned by the highest of authorities in the Executive Branch: the President of the United States.

In the fall of 1991, President Dukakis began a “welfare-to-work” initiative, something that had never been attempted before in any Democratic administration to that date. Modeled off of Dukakis’ programs in his own Massachusetts, it initially had a benign, modest goal: encouraging single mothers to enter the workforce to provide for their families, to break the cycle of government dependency.

This was until the ERTF got ahold of it. In perhaps the most astonishing show of force of the entire Dukakis presidency, they flattered, stalked, and harassed Congress relentlessly until they caved, delivering Dukakis a landmark bill restructuring the AFDC into a series of time-limited blocks, completely transforming how it operated.

Although Dukakis signed the legislation into law citing its merits, he was disturbed by the method through which the ERTF got that law, among others, to his desk. He subsequently signed an executive order disbanding the organization, and Congress acted quickly afterwards to punish the young Boston technocrats for their intrusion deep into the heart of legislative affairs, subpoenaing them to testify before the American people. Dukakis agreed, putting up no struggle save for protecting Bob Reich.

All of this brings us to today. President-elect Rick Santorum has vowed to restore the ERTF and give it a broader mandate, working to slash “liberal” government contracts and promising an “ERTF check” to the American people. Of course, Bob Reich has been quick to oppose this, calling these promises a “total perversion of his vision” and something “the ERTF was never about.” Former President Dukakis joined in too, issuing a joint statement with over 30 former Executive Branch officials in opposition to Santorum’s “downsizing” plans.

As well-intentioned as Dukakis and Reich are, a reminder might serve them both well. The road to hell is, as many say, paved with good intentions.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/TRHXrx7.png'})]) } else if (mondaleDeal == true) { e.pages.push(`

Why Does This Nicaraguan Town Have a Statue of Mike Dukakis in its Town Square?

By James Polansky

U.S. & World News Report | September 11, 2018

At first glance, the town of San Jacinto del Valle might seem like any other small town in Nicaragua. Sprawling through the mountains and valleys of the Central Highlands, the village has corner stores, churches, homes on the forested hillsides, and a nearby reservoir where the locals fish from to support the local economy.

Walking through the town, it’s easy to appreciate the architecture, a mix of local styles as well as Spanish wrought iron and other designs. At the town square, inside a small grassy park, is a cement statue. It depicts a middle-aged man in a suit, smiling, his hands in his pockets. You’d be forgiven for thinking it might be depicting a local politician or community leader. However, a closer look will tell you that this is none other than Michael Dukakis, 41st President of the United States.

So, how did a statue of an American President make its way to the small town of San Jacinto del Valle? It all started in 1989. Dukakis, still in the first year of his presidency, had decided to make good on his promise to negotiate with Gorbachev on the country’s future as the Contras and Sandinistas were duking it out for control of the region. After a series of tense diplomatic meetings, the two leaders strode out hand-in-hand to announce the “Managua Development Accords.”

At first, many Nicaraguans were skeptical that anything would change. And although Moscow would end up walking back many of their commitments after the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, Dukakis kept true to his word, and established vaccination sites, literacy workshops, and job training programs throughout the region by way of USAID.

“I was amazed when he came to visit our town,” said Mrs. Maria Ortega, a clerk at a local supply store, recalling when [Dukakis] visited a local development project. “We’re not a big city, and it was amazing to have a major world leader who saw us as people instead of a dot on a map.” While many share her sentiment, others feel differently about Dukakis’s impact.

“I’m sort of sick of these people coming in,” complained Mr. Juan Castro, a local grocery store owner. “Dukakis is a nice man whose heart was in the right place, but it’s really about influence. This didn’t happen because America is benevolent. I think we should take care of ourselves just like they do.”

71-year-old Victor Lacayo, a former regional director in the Nicaraguan Security Services, personally met the President when he was assigned as part of his local security detail. “I’ll never forget when I first met him,” he reminisced. “I tried my best to speak English to him, and then he responded to me in perfect Spanish. I was amazed.”

Lacayo feels differently about Dukakis and Gorbachev’s framework. “The way I see it, all this help is simply what they owe us after the games we were subjected to, for all those years. So I respect President Dukakis for paying us what we were owed.”

Although opinions differ about the legacy and impact of Dukakis and, more broadly, the Cold War in Nicaragua, the statue enjoys more support than the man. It’s a local icon, and many see it as a symbol of good luck.

Whatever the reason, Mike Dukakis’s goofy smile will be with the people of San Jacinto del Valle for decades to come.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/JDyB0YE.png'})]) } else if (russiaDeal == true) { e.pages.push(`

Russia continues military exercises near Ukrainian border

By David Naughton

BBC | December 9, 2008

According to NATO air defence radars, Russian MiG strike fighters got to within twenty kilometers of Ukrainian airspace during their recent aerial maneuvres, peeling back inland to bomb simulated ground targets set up by the Russian Army. This is only the latest and most daring exploit during Operation Zapad-08, Russia’s newest and boldest military training exercise.

Ostensibly for the purpose of defending Russia from external attack, the exercise is interpreted by many western analysts as only Vladimir Zhirinovsky’s latest encroachment on Ukrainian sovereignty.

The President of Russia has spent the past decade railing against Western influence since the fall of the Soviet Union, crediting it with responsibility for the severe and crippling political failures experienced by the Russian Federation in the 1990s and early 2000s. Indeed, the International Monetary Fund briefly characterized Russia as a “borderline failed state” around the turn of the century, although conditions have since improved.

All of this dysfunction made Zhirinovsky’s appeal highly successful. Post-Soviet Russia has felt thoroughly humiliated and controlled by the West and their lightning-fast NATO expansion. Anti-American sentiment has taken on near-mythical proportions in some regions, with former U.S. Secretary of State Walter Mondale being treated like a boogeyman used to scare children in the same vein as the devil or the Baba Yaga.

Zhirinovsky especially alarmed Western observers with his suggestion that Ukraine “has, on a historical and practical basis, no right to exist as an independent state” during a state dinner last year. Although he had been known for bombastic anti-Western posturing before then, this was the statement that caused NATO observers to seriously consider an attack on Ukraine as an imminent possibility.

“If these are defensive exercises, the proximity to Ukraine raises questions,” said American academic Michael McFaul, rumored to be among President-elect Mark Warner’s top picks for the American ambassadorship to Russia. “It’s something policymakers, including the president elect, will be watching closely.”

More alarmed is the Ukrainian reaction. Kyiv released a statement in response to Operation Zapad-08, denouncing it as “a clear signal of intent for aggression” and calling for greater transparency from Moscow.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/90uyCWn.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dole") { // Beat Dole e.pages.push(`

The Children Yearn For… Mike Dukakis?

By T. J. McManus, Jr.

The Detroit Free Press | December 1, 2025

Michael Dukakis was once nothing to write home about. Despite his two terms as President, he had few achievements to his name, and he spent most of his presidency unpopular with the American public. He has a low-key, quiet personality, few major achievements, and even fewer scandals. Just policy wonkery, an economic recovery, and more of the same Democratic policies that had defined the party for decades up to that point.

So, why is he so popular now?

If you open TikTok these days, you’re bound to see Dukakis edits aplenty. President Dukakis speaking to the 1992 Democratic National Convention, slowed bass-boosted music playing in the background. President Dukakis announcing a “surgical anti-terror campaign” after the World Trade Center attack, spliced with videos of Special Forces operators capturing terrorist leader Usama bin Laden in 1997. There are even AI-generated videos depicting Dukakis as a half-man, half-cyborg, halting criminals and terrorists with terrifying speed and efficiency.

Dukakis nostalgia has also made its way into the mainstream, and has been growing for some time. Last month, Robert Miller penned a column in the New York Times entitled “We Need Another Dukakis.” During last year’s presidential election, Dukakis campaigned for Tom Steyer despite his own age (91 years old!) and has been doing more events at the Dukakis Presidential Center and Library in the Boston suburbs.

On the surface, it might be puzzling that a figure as boring and conventional as Dukakis has become a subject of so much buzz, especially someone with such a boring, dispassionate reputation as him.

But maybe this is exactly why he’s so popular. In a world where President Markwayne Mullin is dominating headlines with controversy, unapologetically bending and breaking democratic norms, that might be exactly what people want. They want a president who is normal. A president who will keep his head down and do his job. A president who projects strength not through his antics and shouting, but his lack thereof. A president who speaks in jobs reports, not gimmicky speeches.

Maybe Americans miss that. And maybe, just maybe, Americans will return to the technocracy they have forsaken over the past decade.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/K29YRZQ.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Robertson") { // Beat Robertson e.pages.push(`

30 Years Later, Campaign Wounds Still Run Deep for Greek-Americans

By Keisha St. James

Inside: New York | September 9, 2022

It has been thirty long years since the 1992 presidential election came and went. America has had many Presidents, many of whom are more famous and more memorable than the mild-mannered Michael Dukakis. But the memories of that campaign, positive and negative, are just like yesterday for many older Greek-Americans here in Queens and around America.

In the 1988 campaign, ethnic politics was mercifully an unspoken issue, despite Dukakis being an Orthodox, Greek-American candidate while opponent George Bush was an Episcopalian of Anglo-Saxon descent. Of course, the ethnic factor existed nonetheless, but Bush never pressed Dukakis on the issue, explicitly or implicitly.

The same did not go for 1992. When Pat Robertson stormed to the Republican nomination, Bible in hand, he wasted no time bringing ethnic and religious politics to the forefront.

“I could not believe it when [Robertson] attacked Dukakis for swearing in on an Orthodox Bible,” said 57-year-old Elizabeth Christodoulou, a proud veteran and second-generation Greek American recalling the controversy. “I was even more shocked when his evangelical supporters marched in lockstep to it. I felt like a stranger in my own country.”

Many of his religious attacks were nowhere near that explicit. Even when Robertson said something as simple as “he’s different from us,” or “he doesn’t share our values,” the undertones were uncomfortably obvious for Greek-Americans.

Fortunately for these communities, Dukakis secured reelection anyways. But the whole episode did not serve as a final victory for Greek-Americans and other ethnics, far from it. It proved that, even if not successfully, politicians still can and will exploit ethnic prejudice to win over voters.

As the years pass, the memory has faded somewhat. Younger Greek-Americans are somewhat more likely to vote Republican than their parents, a rarity in American politics. Christodoulou hopes Greek, Balkan, and Coptic Americans never defect en masse like many other white ethnic groups did during the latter half of the 20th century.

“That election made it clear as day who was on our side,” she sighed. “I hope we never forget it.”

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/OjMXpai.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Buchanan") { // Beat Buchanan e.pages.push(`

IMDb.com | The Duke (2009)

Review by flmwzrd_

Uploaded May 9, 2021

A potentially great film consistently let down by tonal and pacing problems.

The Duke has always been something of a black sheep in Rob Reiner’s filmography. It got plenty of attention when it was released but has since slipped into relative mediocrity, outshone by Reiner classics like When Harry Met Sally, A Few Good Men, and Sleepless In Seattle.

Isn’t that kind of strange? For a film starring Steve Carell, John Goodman, Jack Nicholson, and Gary Oldman, you’d think it’d have a pretty sizable cultural footprint. But it really doesn’t. I did see The Duke in theaters when I was a teenager, but I’ve forgotten so much of it that, upon rewatch, it was more or less watching for the first time.

This, of course, begs the question: Is it any good?

To start out with the least surprising aspect, the actors’ performances are outstanding. You wouldn’t think it, but Steve Carell makes for a great Mike Dukakis. In fact, he uses the relative humorlessness of Dukakis to great effect, giving a totally deadpan performance that is, at times, genuinely hysterical.

Similarly strong is John Goodman as John Sasso. His grounded, down-to-earth demeanor makes for an excellent foil to the aloof, cerebral Dukakis, and he would probably outshine Dukakis’ character if he were portrayed by anyone other than Steve Carell. Jack Nicholson fires on all cylinders as Bob Dole, making an impossibly boring politician seem charismatic and often genuinely menacing, and Gary Oldman practically disappears into his role as Pat Buchanan.

This film can largely be separated into two parts: the first half, which chronicles Dukakis’ comeback reelection bid in 1982 against Ed King, and the second half, which follows Dukakis as President.

Unfortunately, this is where the film begins to lose me. The pacing is good through the first half, but the skip from 1982 to 1988 is disjointed, and the film has to awkwardly detour to 1984 to introduce the audience to Walter Mondale. Dukakis’s first presidential term is also heavily truncated to center the final act on his reelection campaign, stretching three years into barely thirty minutes of screen time.

I would also be remiss if I didn’t point out that this film does little to portray the effect of Dukakis’ actions on his party or his country. It makes no mention of his neoliberal agenda which permanently changed the Democratic Party and paved the way for the hollowing out of the middle class we see today, as well as the Great Recession.

This is what frustrates me. The Duke has the potential to be an outstanding movie. It is inundated with star power, and every piece of dialogue brims with chemistry. But its pacing is too poor and its examination too surface-level to ever truly excel. Maybe its reputation is fitting after all.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/IUBTbf0.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Kemp") { // Beat Kemp e.pages.push(`

Could Riley Moore flip the script in West Virginia?

By Nate Silver

Silver Bulletin | May 23, 2030

West Virginia is among the bluest states in the country. Since the 1960 election, sixteen presidential elections have been held, and in thirteen of those elections, the Democrat has prevailed. The only exceptions to this dominance were Richard Nixon in 1972, Ronald Reagan in 1984, and, most recently, Elizabeth Warren’s 2016 landslide.

Ever since that shocking flip in 2016, Republicans have once again been on the decline in the Mountain State. The state blitzed leftward in 2020 and has since remained firmly in the Democratic column, owing to staunch Democratic support for labor rights, a tradition rooted in the New Deal coalition of the 1930s.

However, Senate Republicans are apparently starting to think that maybe something could happen this time, and their enthusiasm may have basis in fact. For starters, Senator Glenn Elliott’s numbers in West Virginia are softening. Two years ago, he enjoyed a comfortable 62 percent approval rating, now it stands at 54 percent. He has even taken to actively campaigning for votes, something state and local Democrats rarely have to do in West Virginia.

Enter Riley Moore. Once a political unknown with only two years as the Republican state party treasurer under his belt, he has shocked observers with his energetic campaigning. A new Marist poll even shows him trailing Elliott by only a 50-43 margin and 46-48 with undecideds, a shockingly low deficit for a Republican Senate candidate.

Let’s take a look at Riley Moore compared to the last Republican candidate to win any statewide office. Alex Mooney served as West Virginia Secretary of State from 2017 to 2021, vacating his seat to unsuccessfully challenge Steve Williams for the governorship.

To call out the obvious, Mooney ran on a strong Republican ticket led by President Warren, which no doubt played a role in his victory. However, Mooney actually outperformed Warren, winning by 5.2 percentage points compared to her 3.7. Mooney had three advantages: firstly, he had an extremely effective campaign organization, crossover support, and benefited from a poor opponent in Democrat Don Blankenship.

Moore will not enjoy much of what Mooney had. He has no popular president to run alongside, and he is running in an off-year, so turnout is low. In addition, Glenn Elliott is hardly a pushover–he is a formidable politician with a strong record, both electorally and legislatively. But he does share one thing with Mooney: a robust campaign organization. In a small state like West Virginia, where elections can be decided by ground game alone.

Riley Moore is not the favorite in this election, and betting markets currently favor Elliott 92-8. But he is almost certain to put up the best numbers for any Republican statewide since 2016. He may even win outright. But you may not want to bet on it.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/XfE0RtL.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dornan") { // Beat Dornan e.pages.push(`

Former veep Kennedy makes surprise visit at Dukakis Library luncheon

By James Liu

Politics Today | January 29, 2019

Last week, the Dukakis Presidential Center hosted a luncheon in celebration of the 30th anniversary of the first inauguration of President Michael Dukakis. In attendance were numerous Dukakis family friends and politicians, including former Presidents Jim Edgar and John Edwards, as well as former President Dukakis himself.

A surprising attendee was former Vice President John Kennedy, who has, for the most part, retired to a quiet life back in Louisiana, observing the national situation from afar. But here he was among the guests nonetheless, willing to extol his old mentor’s virtues to whoever would listen.

“I’ve always admired how [Dukakis] served our country,” said Kennedy. “He was such an impactful and monumental leader, the first Democrat to serve two terms since FDR, and he really transformed the party. I mean, we didn’t really have two nickels to rub together when Nixon and Reagan ran the show, but he gave us blue dogs a new lease of life. I wouldn’t have been able to serve as Vice President without him.”

Of course, there may be ulterior motives to the former Vice President’s attendance. In this new age of populism and demagoguery, there has been a fresh wave of nostalgia for the former two-term reformer from Brookline, and the old Dukakis ally may be attempting to secure the Duke’s support in case he does decide to throw his hat in the ring and seek the Democratic nomination for President.

Regardless of whether Dukakis gives Kennedy his blessing, Kennedy almost certainly has the support of former President John Edwards, who greeted him warmly at the luncheon. But with Edwards’ tumultuous term still in recent memory, it might be many more years before he is rehabilitated enough to get the same treatment as Michael Dukakis.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/I3613it.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gramm") { // Beat Gramm e.pages.push(`

letterboxd.com | Primary Colors (1995)

Directed by Mike Nichols

“WHAT WENT DOWN ON THE WAY TO THE TOP.”

An aloof New England governor named Matthew Dirkman is running for President of the United States. He has to contend with his cutthroat campaign manager, Jack Stark, his advisors Peter Bowling and Kent O’Connell, and rival candidates Alan Graham and Joseph Jefferson. As they get further and further into the primary contest, the Governor’s team races to count delegates, secure endorsements, and keep the ship together in the wild world of party politicking.

147 minutes · Universal Pictures

Popular Reviews

Review by allen ford ⭐⭐⭐⭐ ❤️
“The best America is… what is it again? Coming?”
I couldn’t believe it when I learned that the hilarious sequence of screw-ups that ended up putting Senator Brandon’s plagiarism tape in Stark’s hands was actually based on true events. With retrospect, it’s hard to believe there was a time when something like that constituted a serious scandal. How times have changed.

Review by TedThing ⭐⭐⭐½
Every time I watch this, I notice something new. The script is just that good. 32 years after this movie’s release, it still has something interesting to say: about race and sex in politics, money in our campaigns, you name it. Should be required watching for any young person who wants to get into government.

Review by rockl4nd ⭐⭐⭐
I like the parallels between the in-universe candidates and the real-life ones. My dad volunteered for Al Gore in ‘88. I often think about what would have happened if we got a southern, moderate Democrat instead of Dukakis. The DLC could have transformed the country. Instead we just got more of the same.

Review by flowersinthesnow ⭐⭐⭐⭐½ ❤️
god this movie is so good i wish matthew dirkman was a real president

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/xpCTlAd.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gingrich") { // Beat Gingrich e.pages.push(`

The West Wing Characters and their Real Life Counterparts

By Matt Walsh

The Huffington Post | December 8, 2012

It’s little secret that the hit political drama The West Wing takes inspiration from real life politics. As rumors circulate about a potential NBC miniseries following Sam Seaborn running for President and how it might handle the incoming administration of President-elect Barbara Boxer. With that in mind, let’s take a trip down memory lane and look at how The West Wing handled their real-life counterparts during the presidency of Michael Dukakis.

Will Bailey: Leslie Dach
Will Bailey (Joshua Malina) served as the press secretary for almost the entirety of the 7 seasons of The West Wing, making him the most often recurring cast member in the series (besides the President himself). Leslie Dach had a similarly long tenure as Michael Dukakis’ press secretary, both in his first and second terms. Dach’s reputation for unflappability was heavily reflected in Bailey, despite his many personal missteps and scandals throughout the course of the show. At the end of the series, Bailey is seen in his role as a Congressman, the same path that Dach took.

Claudia Cregg: Susan Estrich
Claudia Jean Cregg (Allison Janney) served as White House counsel for most of The West Wing, interspersed in Season 7 after being nominated as Attorney General. Cregg was inspired by several individuals - most notably Supreme Court Justice Janet Reno - but “nobody” was more pivotal in the construction of Cregg’s character than Attorney General Susan Estrich, according to show creator Aaron Sorkin. Cregg was selected as White House Counsel early instead of Attorney General because Sorkin wanted to emphasize the inner workings of government, “even smaller than the cabinet.” Cregg’s laser-focus and competence made her a staple of the intrigue that so many West Wing fans fell in love with.

Toby Ziegler: Paul Brountas
Toby Ziegler (Richard Schiff) serves as Director of the Office of Legislative Affairs, the position that handles outreach with Congress. Though White House counsel Paul Brountas never had this role in the Dukakis administration, show writer Lawrence O’Donnell recalls that “For Dukakis, Paul Brountas was equal to, if not above John Sasso [the Chief of Staff] in influence.” Though often moody and occasionally explosive while attempting to keep the caucus in line, Ziegler was designed as an intellectual on par with the President, similar to how close Brountas was to Dukakis across their shared careers, and a strong idealist under the surface.

Josh Lyman: Kirk O’Donnell
Josh Lyman (Bradley Whitford) is the Deputy Chief of Staff, a famously witty cast member who brought brevity to a series defined by its sharpness and seriousness. Lyman was never comic relief, however, serving as a key member of the President’s “Three Musketeers,” including Toby and Sam, much like his real-life inspiration of Kirk O’Donnell, a key Dukakis advisor. Whitford’s performance eventually won him an Emmy, in large part thanks to his impassioned monologue in Season 2, Episode 10, Oh, Rosemary!

Sam Seaborn: John Sasso
Sam Seaborn (Rob Lowe) is the President’s ruthless Chief of Staff and a longtime friend who helped manage his campaigns for President and as governor of New Hampshire. Lowe won his first Emmy during Season 5, when he portrayed Seaborn under investigation for leaking ethics complaints about the show’s infamous antagonist, Speaker Glenn Walken (John Goodman), during the 2002 election. Seaborn would eventually resign because of the investigation, coinciding with Lowe’s departure from the series. Sasso, on the other hand, remained at the core of the Dukakis administration for both terms before retiring from politics.

Leo McGarry: Lloyd Bentsen
Leonard McGarry (John Spencer) is the Vice President, a former Air Force pilot, cabinet secretary, and Senator, who is a close advisor to the President and an “upright, moral counterweight in a political world that is often hellish and chaotic,” said Sorkin. “Leo” was also often stated by the show writers to be a conciliatory check on Sam’s style of politics. Lloyd Bentsen served a similar role, although famously kept mostly to himself as Dukakis’ Vice President.

Bernie Ripken: Michael Dukakis
President Bernard Ripken (George Clooney) “wasn’t intended as a caricature of Michael Dukakis,” said executive producer Tommy Schlamme. “When Aaron and I first talked about it, we wanted a Kennedy-esque figure at the helm, believing that he would be someone above all the infighting in politics. But he eventually became more like Dukakis … someone calculated, lonely, lethal.” This character archetype was a big change for Clooney, who had heretofore never played such characters. He eventually had a high profile meeting with Michael Dukakis by way of Olympia, the former President’s cousin who made appearances in the show, which he described as “transformative” in preparing him for the role.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/8UKFWod.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'win' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Rumsfeld") { // Beat Rumsfeld e.pages.push(`

Baghdad defies Gulf nations, will continue military exercises

By William Allen

Associated Press | November 29, 1996

In a rare show of Arab unity, the Gulf Nations alongside Egypt and Syria issued a combined rebuke of Iraq’s continued military buildup along the Saudi border. This expansion comes alongside continuing air exercises that Saddam has described as preparations for “The Mother of All Battles.”

The buildup has alarmed Republicans and Democrats alike, with Senator Bob Graham insisting Washington act to stabilize the region before it’s too late. “It’s concerning,” said Graham, ranking member of the Senate Intelligence Committee, “After 1990, nobody in Riyadh can afford to assume Iraqi exercises are harmless.” Senator Joe Biden offered choice words about the pronounced unwillingness of the US to defend the now-exiled Emir of Kuwait. “This is, in full and plain form, a direct violation of the very idea of the founding charter of the United Nations. Saddam must be brought to heel for the crimes he has committed, both against his own people and against the entirety of the region.”

Saddam, however, insists that his military buildup is entirely within Iraq’s rights as a sovereign state, with state media and government officials downplaying the unity among regional powers acting against them. Baghdad thus far has insisted that this coalition, primarily spearheaded by the United Arab Emirates, and the Kuwaiti government in exile and joined by Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, Syria and Egypt, is ultimately powerless without American backing. Iraqi state media has alleged “Zionist collaboration” and “extreme fragility that will be shattered by the very first ballistic missile on their pathetic fiefdoms.”

The White House is trying to stake out a middle ground. “With regards to the aggression of Iraq on neighboring states, we are confident that our existing diplomatic response to Iraqi aggression, including United Nations sanctions and no-fly zones, is a sufficient check on their regional ambitions,” the outgoing Dukakis administration stated in a recent press release. “However, should Iraq act in a way that further harms regional stability such as attacking Saudi Arabia, we will respond effectively and appropriately, including a potential military response.”

filmgeeks.com | What do you guys think about the new Dukakis movie in development?

Apparently Timothee Chalamet is President Dukakis and Bryan Cranston is Ed King. I’m kind of split on it. On one hand, we’ve never really gotten a good movie about their rivalry, just the 2010 miniseries.

transformersfan_1984 • September 29, 2027 17:35:16

Responses (show all)

riseup1966 • September 29, 2027 17:59:02
Always loved Bryan Cranston. Excited for it.

CognitoStultus • September 29, 2027 18:15:48
It looks okay I guess. I’m kind of upset that that feud ended the way it did. My mom was a really big King supporter in ‘92, my family never cared for Dukakis. Or Bentsen, for that matter

ifubuildit • September 29, 2027 18:44:59
I wonder how they’ll handle Robert Reich and the ERTF. You have kind of a dilemma there, ethically speaking. They abused their power and set a precedent, but it objectively helped Dukakis in many of his major initiatives.

James Lockhart • September 29, 2027 17:45:22
I doubt they’d do a better job than The Duke (2009), that’s a hugely underrated movie.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/HghI832.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dole") { // Lose to Dole e.pages.push(`

The Year the Keys Broke

By Ketric Edelman

Current Events with Ketric | October 22, 2016

As polls show a tightening race between President Russ Feingold and Senator Carly Fiorina with barely more than two weeks before Election Day, it seems like every predictor and their brother is hedging their bets. Nobody (including me) wants to be caught flat-footed if they make a prediction one way and it goes the other, and the polling gap in almost every key swing state is within two points, easily within the margin-of-error of modern pollsters. Nobody, that is, except Allan Lichtman.

Lichtman, a 69-year-old professor at American University, devised the “Keys to the White House” in 1982 alongside Vladimir Keilis-Borok as a way to predict the outcome of United States presidential elections using thirteen true-or-false conditional statements judging the strength of the incumbent party.

He is boldly predicting that Russ Feingold will secure a second term on November 8, and his record since the 1984 election is perfect. Well, perfect save for one year.

In 1992, President Michael Dukakis went against then-Senator Bob Dole with a poor economy and foreign upheaval. Lichtman surprised observers when he, against the grain, came out and definitively declared that Dukakis would indeed secure reelection against Dole, having secured nine out of the thirteen keys.

There’s only one problem—he didn’t. Dole won the election, and went on to serve as America’s 42nd President.

Lichtman has explained over and over that there were circumstances unique to the 1992 election that caused the keys to fail, and that the system overall is still perfectly sound after unspecified “changes” he made to the system between the 1992 and 1996 elections. And, to his credit, he has since predicted every election successfully, even 2004, which most observers predicted incorrectly.

Even so, there are many parallels between 1992 and 2016. Both see an embattled Democratic incumbent emblematic of traditional Democratic ideals running against a pro-business Republican challenger, with foreign policy and economic uncertainty taking a front seat to cultural issues.

Could the keys fail again? It’s a real possibility. Even if Allan Lichtman rejects the possibility out of hand.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/tg1KXvE.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Robertson") { // Lose to Robertson e.pages.push(`

The Reformer: Michael Dukakis and the End of a Dream | Hardcover

By Kai Bird (author)

Released May 9, 2013
(4.5) ⭐⭐⭐⭐½ (772)

“Important and necessary… [a] historic presidential biography… Bird is able to build a persuasive case that the Dukakis presidency deserves a new look.” — The New York Times Book Review

Twenty years after President Michael Dukakis left office, many have already forgotten his presidency. Sandwiched between the memorable Ronald Reagan and the hell-raising Pat Robertson, the mild-mannered Dukakis is often omitted entirely, even when Jimmy Carter is remembered fondly by many. In reality, though, the Duke’s presidency was anything but forgettable.

With pinpoint accuracy and attention to detail, Bird takes you through the key decision points of the Dukakis presidency. He gives you fly-on-the-wall accounts of Dukakis’ most famous struggles: the John Sasso controversy, the struggle for healthcare, his clashes with Congress, the collapse of the Soviet Union, his campaign for reelection, and so much more. He even shines light on aspects of the Dukakis presidency previously unbeknownst to history, examining his Federal Reserve deliberations, his economic policy task force, and his campaign meetings.

Not only that, but Bird even takes you through the key events in Dukakis’ life that shaped the leader he became. Starting during Dukakis’s Depression-era childhood, he shows you how Dukakis’s immigrant parents and older brother shaped the man he became. He takes you through Dukakis’s military service and his time in the state legislature, standing up to a formidable political machine led by Speaker Joseph “Iron Duke” Cannon. He takes you through his statewide campaigns, including his humiliating defeat—and shocking comeback—against Governor Edward King, leading to his ascendance in national politics.

Bird is quick to challenge the common memory of Dukakis being a bland, forgettable president. He does it so effectively that, should you not be careful, you might find yourself wishing for that second term the man himself so coveted.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/cF4Tcay.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Buchanan") { // Lose to Buchanan e.pages.push(`

Paul rallies voters despite dismal polling

By Ketric Anderson

Pensacola News Journal | September 24, 2012

A new poll released on Friday morning by CBS News paints a dismal picture for supporters of President Ron Paul: he trails Democratic nominee Jerome Powell by 16 percentage points with registered voters, and by 20 points with likely voters, Furthermore, 54 percent of Americans polled named Democratic running mate Ted Strickland as the more qualified candidate for Vice President, while 42 percent named Republican running mate, former Governor Gary Johnson.

There is no sugarcoating that it has been a difficult presidential term for President Paul. After barely winning the Presidency over then-Vice President Blanche Lincoln in the throes of the housing market crash, Paul had to put up with a Congress largely hostile to his agenda. Even former President Pat Buchanan, whose 1992 victory largely began the trend towards “Republican Jacobinism” in the GOP, has offered tepid support at best to the embattled President.

Indeed, he has used the veto 406 times during his presidency, 352 of them being overridden by bipartisan majorities in each houses of Congress. He was also impeached after refusing to appoint a Federal Reserve chair, though the Senate fell two votes short of convicting him. He has lost almost all institutional support, most of his own cabinet, and his own Vice President.

He now faces Jerome Powell, a central banker and former Treasury Secretary during President Gephardt’s second term, who is not a registered Democrat and has entered the political sphere solely out of disdain for Paul’s political project. No poll has marked the President above Powell, and the overwhelming majority depict a double-digit gap between the two of them.

Even so, Paul remains undeterred. “Paul has campaigned with more energy and more vigor than any point before in his history,” mentioned a Republican strategist ambivalent about the President. “He’s building real enthusiasm, and not just from partisan attacks. His anti-establishment argument still resonates.”

“We’re never going to let central banking take over our country,” proclaimed a defiant Paul to a crowd of supporters in Amarillo, Texas. “I know we’re going to stop Jerome Powell. Momentum is on our side, and the only poll that counts is on Election Day.”

So far, this supposed groundswell has not materialized in general election polling, and it is unclear if it ever will. Regardless, one thing should be abundantly clear: Ron Paul is not going down without a fight.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/I1eiLom.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Kemp") { // Lose to Kemp e.pages.push(`

Is this the first politically-charged Super Bowl in American history?

By Scott Fitzsimmons

ESPN | February 3, 2027

In January of 2017, when then-President Michelle Bachmann wrote a complaint to the National Football League after witnessing a league-sponsored message in support of gay pride while attending that year’s NFC championship game, it ignited a firestorm in sports and broader culture over the place of companies in the broader debate over social issues.

Now, ten years later, sports leagues, especially the NFL, are as politicized as ever. But not on any cultural issues. Indeed, social progressivism has largely won out, and LGBTQ individuals largely enjoy more rights than they ever have before. The debate now centers on something entirely different: Fiscal policy.

In the tense closing minutes of this year’s Wild Card round playoff game against their rival New England Patriots, a group of rowdy Bills fans unfurled a flag depicting the face of 42nd U.S. President and former Bills quarterback Jack Kemp. Within seconds, all of Highmark Stadium was chanting in unison: “For Kemp! For Kemp!”

Indeed, the Bills have established themselves as one of the foremost supply-sider teams in the country, largely because of President Kemp’s legacy. Their staff, fans, and even players make no secret of their support for low marginal tax rates and an expansionary fiscal policy.

“It’s ridiculous to think that raising taxes would increase government revenue,” tweeted longtime Bills quarterback Josh Allen last October. “Labor supply is elastic, and the increase in productivity from cutting taxes would actually boost revenue.”

Of course, this did not come without controversy. “Totally ridiculous,” quote-tweeted Caleb Williams, young starting quarterback of the Chicago Bears. “That is an economic falsehood. Ask Sam Brownback how that went.”

The Chicago Bears have become one of the most prominent deficit hawk teams in the country. Many players have begun to put the “#PayYourTaxes” hashtag in their social media bios, and their alternate uniforms this year even depicted a dollar sign with a lock symbol over it on their sleeves, emblematic of the team’s devotion to austerity.

Josh Allen and Caleb Williams had no way of knowing that their brief spat on Twitter would foreshadow their teams going against each other in the Super Bowl. As both of these teams vie for long-lost glory, economics and the legacy of President Kemp are sure to be a hot topic among fans of these teams and the broader league.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/CAxjsqK.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Dornan") { // Lose to Dornan e.pages.push(`

Cohen, Ferraro “Duke” it out in New York

By Peter K. Breshnahan

CQ Roll Call | January 3, 2000

Exactly one year from today, there is a possibility that either Michael Cohen or Geraldine Ferraro will be inaugurated as New York’s next United States Senator. Whether it will be Cohen or Ferraro depends on the results of the April 28 primary.

Central to this contest is the legacy of President Michael Dukakis. Though both candidates are veterans of the Dukakis administration, they handle it in entirely different ways. Geraldine Ferraro, former congresswoman, vice presidential candidate, and Dukakis NATO Ambassador, prefers to downplay her connection to the former President.

“This isn’t about Dukakis, or Dornan, or whoever,” she said flatly during a campaign speech in Queens early December. “It’s about working people. It’s about all of you, who have been ripped off by an establishment that has long lost its way.” Ferraro is teetering close to outright rejecting President Dukakis’s legacy, a common perspective in the Democratic Party after his neoliberal tendencies led to Bob Dornan winning the White House.

Michael Cohen takes a different approach. The 34-year-old New York attorney worked as a campaign lawyer for President Dukakis during his 1992 reelection campaign and has since leveraged those connections to make a name for himself in New York Democratic politics.

“I will never apologize for defending President Dukakis,” he proclaimed during a speech of his own in Long Island. “He was dealt a bad deck of cards and played them the best he could. He was given an economy tattered by Reaganomics and had to make do with it. In my house, he will always be a hero.”

Though Dukakis is, on the balance, not fondly-remembered, Cohen is banking on the existence of untapped goodwill for the 41st President. New York is a liberal-leaning state, and its electoral votes helped put Dukakis in the White House in 1988. And according to a recent New York University poll, Cohen’s message might be resonating. He still trails Ferraro, but the gap has shrunk to nine percentage points, 47 percent to 38 percent, down from fourteen percentage points three weeks ago.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/4gjJIyG.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gramm") { // Lose to Gramm e.pages.push(`

Dukakis presidential library opens in Brookline

By Maurice Elliott

Boston Daily News | January 28, 2000

After years of uncertainty and trouble raising funds, the Michael and Kitty Dukakis Presidential Center finally opened today. The entire complex is designed with a mixture of classical and modernist architecture and houses the entirety of Dukakis’ presidential records, which were initially retained by Northeastern University and are estimated to be about thirteen million pages in total length.

In addition to records storage, the center is also home to the Michael S. Dukakis Presidential Museum, dedicated to “telling the story of the Dukakis presidency, one of the most important in modern history,” as well as “exhibiting artifacts and exhibits from President Dukakis’s life, campaigns, governorship, and presidency.”

During the opening ceremony, the attendance was smaller than the event coordinators had originally intended for. This did not deter a happy Dukakis, who joked during his set of remarks that “that just means I get to take home all the donuts and coffee.”

After the ceremony, former White House Chief of Staff John Sasso seemed optimistic about the Dukakis library. “It’s a real state of the art facility,” he commented, “and museum too. I think people who come here are going to learn so much about Mike Dukakis’s transformational presidency.” Sasso could not stay long during the reception. He had to drive back to New Hampshire; he is managing Senator John Kerry’s campaign in that state and the primary takes place in three days’ time.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/01OOhKs.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Gingrich") { // Lose to Gingrich e.pages.push(`

An Oral History with Former House Speaker David Bonior

Columbia Digital Collections

Name
Bonior, David E. (David Edward), 1933- (Interviewee)
Matthews, Jeffrey H., 1974-2023 (Interviewer)
Archival Context
Series IV: Columbia University. Subseries IV.8: Congressional History
Date/Language
2010 September 21, English

Interview Transcript

(interview begins mid-conversation)

MATTHEWS: —you were what, Minority Whip during the Dukakis presidency?

BONIOR: I was. For the latter two years, at least. I went back to the majority after the ‘94 midterms, and to the Speakership two years later.

MATTHEWS: Did you ever get the sense that President Dukakis was… able and effective in how he worked with Congress?

BONIOR: I mean, we could have got a lot more done, during the first two years of his presidency. The 101st Congress. But he was a very nice man.

MATTHEWS: But it was better than during Gingrich’s presidency.

BONIOR: Christ, yes.

MATTHEWS: What was that like?

BONIOR: For the first two years, it was miserable. I mean, you’re in the minority. So all you can do is stand aside while he, you know—

MATTHEWS: Yeah.

BONIOR: —but it got better after ‘94. We retook Congress and flipped the script on him.

MATTHEWS: What kind of effect on politics do you think that… period of time had?

BONIOR: Well, it was huge. It transformed the nature of congressional relations and started the pipeline of Speakers seeking the Presidency. That was never a thing before, now it’s as common or more common as, you know, governors or senators running. The Speaker, or Minority Leader when in the minority, has essentially become what the Leader of the Opposition is in the United Kingdom.

MATTHEWS: I take it that this bothers you?

BONIOR: It does. The Speakership isn’t a stepping stone and it demeans the office to treat it like that. I was one of the last Speakers to actually, you know, respect the office, myself and Nancy Pelosi. And this goes for both parties.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/YnbzCjl.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "Rumsfeld") { // Lose to Rumsfeld e.pages.push(`

Rubio Finally Opens the Rumsfeld Presidential Playbook

By Malcolm Ramsay

Jacobin | September 12, 2017

Days before his invasion of Iraq under the pretext of liberating Kuwait, President Donald Rumsfeld declared a crusade to “end the age of despotism in the Middle East.” Today, Marco Rubio echoes the same rhetoric to topple the Iranian government.

The Earth is once more polluted by the same familiar sound of 1994. Computers humming, statisticians typing, carriers positioned on the war room table. The cost of war costs were calculated and the troops prepared for orders. In less than a week, Iraq’s armed forces had entirely been decimated. 32 minutes after the order had been given, President Donald Rumsfeld addressed the nation to justify the conflict. His speech decried the imperial conquest of Kuwait, Saddam’s utter barbaric brutality, and Rumsfeld’s own conviction that such a conflict was the beginning of a new age of democracy in the Middle East. A little over twenty years later a new President uses the same template.

President Rubio addressed the nation last night, his speech full of the same familiar music. The difference? Little. Instead of decrying the barbarism of Saddam Hussein, President Rubio decried the rulership of “pure theocracy” and the radical eschatology of the Iranian regime. His claims about a potential Iranian nuclear weapons program gave way to fear-mongering, that this regime may be prepared to bring about the end times for its faith. These weapons he claimed, besides threatening the West, guaranteed the oppression of millions of women, ethnic, and religious minorities. He concluded that such a regime was a threat to civilization itself, and proclaimed that a new age of Iranian democracy would be born.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/Q32GOup.png'})]) } else if (finalOutcome == 'loss' && campaignTrail_temp.candidate_json[0]["fields"].last_name == "King") { // Lose to King e.pages.push(`

The Blows Keep Coming for Ed King

By James Rizzo, Jr.

Beltway Today | January 27, 1993

Yesterday, a discouraged President-elect Edward King announced the withdrawal of Sen. Bob Packwood’s nomination for Secretary of the Treasury, only the latest in a string of stumbles related to King’s cabinet nominees.

Hopes were initially high for Packwood’s confirmation, with Republican insiders labeling him as a “valuable bridge-builder” with “just the insider chops an outsider like [President] King needs” after the King transition team announced his appointment last year.

However, a series of bombshells have detonated since November of 1992, beginning with a Washington Post exposé detailing claims of sexual assault and abuse over his career from ten women. In the following weeks, more women came forward, raising the total number of accusers to nineteen. Further controversy resulted from the status of Packwood’s diary, with Senate Democrats suggesting that it was subpoenable.

Although King was, at first, insistent on keeping the Senator on board, even suggesting to supporters that subpoenaing Packwood’s diary was “impossible” because “the Fifth Amendment bars self-incrimination.” But as the claims mounted through January, King had no choice but to cut the senator loose.

As rumors swirl over whether Packwood should resign his seat entirely, the King Administration has moved on, just as it moved on from nominee Rep. Dan Burton’s multitude of ethical controversies after his nomination to the United Nations ambassadorship, and Rep. Henry Hyde’s protracted extramarital affair.

`) //e.image = "" e.executable.push([2, (() => {e.image = e.image = 'https://itsastronomical.com/assets/1992Duke/imgur/r3ZWQqQ.png'})]) } else { e.pages.push(`

Fallback Ending

I don't know how you got here.

let processedNodes = new Set(); async function secondMutation(mutationsList, observer) { if (newobserverRunning) return; newobserverRunning = true; // stop observing observer.disconnect(); // addScrollbar const overallResult = document.getElementById('overall_result'); const state_result = document.getElementById('state_result'); if (overallResult && !processedNodes.has(overallResult)) { overallResult.style.overflow = 'auto'; const buttons = document.querySelectorAll('#view_electoral_map, #answer_select_button, #ok_button, #final_election_map_button'); const handleClick = () => { overallResult.style.overflow = 'auto'; }; buttons.forEach(button => button.addEventListener('click', handleClick)); processedNodes.add(overallResult); } if (state_result && !processedNodes.has(state_result)) { state_result.style.overflow = 'auto'; const buttons = document.querySelectorAll('#view_electoral_map, #answer_select_button, #ok_button, #final_election_map_button'); const handleClick = () => { state_result.style.overflow = 'auto'; }; buttons.forEach(button => button.addEventListener('click', handleClick)); processedNodes.add(state_result); } // Resume observing observer.observe(document.documentElement, { childList: true, subtree: true }); newobserverRunning = false; } let newObserver = new MutationObserver(secondMutation); newObserver.observe(document.documentElement, { childList: true, subtree: true }); // The data visualizer from 2012LBM (function () { const VISUALIZATION_MODES = { SOLID: 'solid', MARGINS: 'margins', VOTE_SHARE: 'vote_share', CANDIDATE_STRENGTH: 'candidate_strength', COMPETITIVENESS: 'competitiveness', TURNOUT_CHANGE: 'turnout_change', TIPPING_POINT: 'tipping_point', ISSUE_STANCE: 'issue_stance', ISSUE_WEIGHT: 'issue_weight', CANDIDATE_ISSUE_ALIGNMENT: 'candidate_issue_alignment', COALITION_BUILDER: 'coalition_builder', CANDIDATE_COMBINER: 'candidate_combiner', SENATE_RACES: 'senate_races', HOUSE_COMPOSITION: 'house_composition' }; let currentMode = VISUALIZATION_MODES.SOLID; let selectedCandidateIndex = 0; let candidatePerformanceMode = 'absolute'; let styleCache = {}; let isInitialized = false; let candidateMaxPercentages = {}; let stateDataCache = {}; let selectedIssueIndex = 0; let selectedAlignmentCandidateIndex = 0; let alignmentComparisonMode = 'single'; let selectedComparisonCandidates = [0, 1, 2, 3]; let coalitionCandidateIndex = 0; let coalitionVoteShare = 0.5; let coalitionMode = 'solid'; let coalitionResults = {}; let candidates = {}; let combinedCandidateIndex1 = 0; let combinedCandidateIndex2 = 2; let combinedCandidateIndex3 = -1; let combinerMode = 'two'; let combinerResults = {}; let showAverageIssueStance = false; let headToHeadMode = false; let headToHeadTeam1Index1 = 0; let headToHeadTeam1Index2 = 1; let headToHeadTeam2Index1 = 2; let headToHeadTeam2Index2 = 3; let headToHeadResults = {}; let legendPosition = null; let legendMoved = false; const colorCache = {}; const stateUpdateQueue = []; let animationFrameRequested = false; let dragState = { isDragging: false, currentElement: null, startX: 0, startY: 0, startLeft: 0, startTop: 0 }; const SENATE_INCUMBENT_PARTIES = { 'AZ': 'R', 'CA': 'D', 'CT': 'I', 'DE': 'D', 'FL': 'D', 'HI': 'D', 'IN': 'R', 'ME': 'R', 'MD': 'D', 'MA': 'R', 'MI': 'D', 'MN': 'D', 'MS': 'R', 'MO': 'D', 'MT': 'D', 'NE': 'D', 'NV': 'R', 'NJ': 'D', 'NM': 'D', 'NY': 'D', 'ND': 'D', 'OH': 'D', 'PA': 'D', 'RI': 'D', 'TN': 'R', 'TX': 'R', 'UT': 'R', 'VT': 'I', 'VA': 'D', 'WA': 'D', 'WV': 'D', 'WI': 'D', 'WY': 'R' }; function makeDraggable(element, handle = null) { const dragHandle = handle || element; dragHandle.style.cursor = 'move'; if (!handle) { element.style.borderTop = '3px solid #007acc'; } else { handle.style.background = 'linear-gradient(135deg, #f0f0f0 0%, #e0e0e0 100%)'; handle.style.borderBottom = '1px solid #ccc'; } function startDrag(e) { e.preventDefault(); e.stopPropagation(); dragState.isDragging = true; dragState.currentElement = element; const rect = element.getBoundingClientRect(); dragState.startX = e.clientX; dragState.startY = e.clientY; dragState.startLeft = rect.left; dragState.startTop = rect.top; element.style.position = 'absolute'; element.style.left = rect.left + 'px'; element.style.top = rect.top + 'px'; element.style.right = 'auto'; element.style.bottom = 'auto'; element.style.zIndex = '10001'; element.style.opacity = '0.9'; document.addEventListener('mousemove', drag); document.addEventListener('mouseup', stopDrag); document.body.style.userSelect = 'none'; } function drag(e) { if (!dragState.isDragging || dragState.currentElement !== element) return; e.preventDefault(); const deltaX = e.clientX - dragState.startX; const deltaY = e.clientY - dragState.startY; const newLeft = dragState.startLeft + deltaX; const newTop = dragState.startTop + deltaY; const elementRect = element.getBoundingClientRect(); const maxLeft = window.innerWidth - elementRect.width; const maxTop = window.innerHeight - elementRect.height; const constrainedLeft = Math.max(0, Math.min(newLeft, maxLeft)); const constrainedTop = Math.max(0, Math.min(newTop, maxTop)); element.style.left = constrainedLeft + 'px'; element.style.top = constrainedTop + 'px'; } function stopDrag(e) { if (!dragState.isDragging || dragState.currentElement !== element) return; dragState.isDragging = false; dragState.currentElement = null; element.style.zIndex = '1000'; element.style.opacity = '1'; document.removeEventListener('mousemove', drag); document.removeEventListener('mouseup', stopDrag); document.body.style.userSelect = ''; if (element.id === 'map_legend' || element.id === 'map_controls') { const rect = element.getBoundingClientRect(); const scrollTop = document.documentElement.scrollTop || document.body.scrollTop; if (element.id === 'map_legend') { legendPosition = { left: rect.left, top: rect.top + scrollTop }; legendMoved = true; } } } dragHandle.addEventListener('mousedown', startDrag); dragHandle.addEventListener('touchstart', function (e) { const touch = e.touches[0]; const mouseEvent = new MouseEvent('mousedown', { clientX: touch.clientX, clientY: touch.clientY }); startDrag(mouseEvent); }); document.addEventListener('touchmove', function (e) { if (dragState.isDragging && dragState.currentElement === element) { const touch = e.touches[0]; const mouseEvent = new MouseEvent('mousemove', { clientX: touch.clientX, clientY: touch.clientY }); drag(mouseEvent); e.preventDefault(); } }); document.addEventListener('touchend', function (e) { if (dragState.isDragging && dragState.currentElement === element) { const mouseEvent = new MouseEvent('mouseup', {}); stopDrag(mouseEvent); } }); } function hexToRgb(hex) { const normalized = hex.replace(/^#/, ''); if (colorCache[normalized]?.rgb) { return colorCache[normalized].rgb; } const num = parseInt(normalized, 16); const rgb = [(num >> 16) & 0xff, (num >> 8) & 0xff, num & 0xff]; if (!colorCache[normalized]) colorCache[normalized] = {}; colorCache[normalized].rgb = rgb; return rgb; } function rgbToHex(r, g, b) { r = Math.max(0, Math.min(255, Math.round(r))); g = Math.max(0, Math.min(255, Math.round(g))); b = Math.max(0, Math.min(255, Math.round(b))); const key = `${r},${g},${b}`; if (colorCache[key] && colorCache[key].hex) { return colorCache[key].hex; } const hex = `#${r.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')}${g.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')}${b.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')}`; if (!colorCache[key]) colorCache[key] = {}; colorCache[key].hex = hex; return hex; } function interpolateColor(c1, c2, factor = 0.5) { if (factor <= 0) return c1; if (factor >= 1) return c2; return c1.map((v, i) => Math.round(v + factor * (c2[i] - v))); } /* function varyHexColor(hex, variationFrac = 0.05) { const rgb = hexToRgb(hex); const newRgb = rgb.map(channel => { const delta = (Math.random() * 2 - 1) * variationFrac * 255; return Math.max(0, Math.min(255, Math.round(channel + delta))); }); return rgbToHex(...newRgb); } */ function blendMarginColor(baseColor, margin) { const cacheKey = `margin_${baseColor}_${(margin * 1000 | 0)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; const baseRGB = hexToRgb(baseColor); const whiteRGB = [255, 255, 255]; let blendFactor; if (margin < 0.01) { blendFactor = 0.15; } else if (margin < 0.05) { blendFactor = 0.15 + (margin - 0.01) * 8; } else if (margin < 0.10) { blendFactor = 0.47 + (margin - 0.05) * 5; } else if (margin < 0.15) { blendFactor = 0.72 + (margin - 0.10) * 3; } else { blendFactor = 0.87 + Math.min((margin - 0.15) * 1.3, 0.13); } const blendedRGB = interpolateColor(whiteRGB, baseRGB, blendFactor); const result = rgbToHex(...blendedRGB); colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function blendPerformanceColor(baseColor, percent, candidateData) { const cacheKey = `perf_${baseColor}_${percent.toFixed(4)}_${candidateData.max.toFixed(4)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; const baseRGB = hexToRgb(baseColor); const maxPct = candidateData.max; const avgPct = candidateData.avg; const p90 = candidateData.p90; const p10 = candidateData.p10; const candidatePk = Object.keys(candidateMaxPercentages).find(pk => candidateMaxPercentages[pk] === candidateData ); const hasElectoralVotes = campaignTrail_temp.final_overall_results .find(r => r.candidate == candidatePk)?.electoral_votes > 0; let targetRGB; let intensity; if (!hasElectoralVotes || maxPct <= 0.15) { const normalizedPct = percent / Math.max(maxPct, 0.001); if (normalizedPct > 0.85) { targetRGB = baseRGB; intensity = 0.9; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.70) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 15)); intensity = 0.75; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.55) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 35)); intensity = 0.60; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.40) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 60)); intensity = 0.45; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.25) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 90)); intensity = 0.35; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.10) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 120)); intensity = 0.25; } else { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 150)); intensity = 0.18; } } else if (maxPct > 0.75 && maxPct - p90 > 0.15) { if (percent > p90) { targetRGB = baseRGB; intensity = 0.95; } else if (percent > avgPct) { const lightBaseRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 30)); targetRGB = lightBaseRGB; const positionInUpperRange = (p90 > avgPct) ? (percent - avgPct) / (p90 - avgPct) : 1; intensity = 0.6 + (positionInUpperRange * 0.3); } else if (percent > p10) { const veryLightRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 80)); targetRGB = veryLightRGB; const positionInLowerRange = (avgPct > p10) ? (percent - p10) / (avgPct - p10) : 1; intensity = 0.3 + (positionInLowerRange * 0.25); } else { const pastelRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 120)); targetRGB = pastelRGB; intensity = 0.2 + (p10 > 0 ? (percent / p10) * 0.15 : 0); } } else if (maxPct > 0.40) { const normalizedPct = percent / maxPct; if (normalizedPct > 0.8) { targetRGB = baseRGB; intensity = 0.85 + (normalizedPct - 0.8) * 0.75; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.6) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 20)); intensity = 0.7 + (normalizedPct - 0.6) * 0.75; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.4) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 50)); intensity = 0.5 + (normalizedPct - 0.4) * 1.0; } else if (normalizedPct > 0.2) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 80)); intensity = 0.3 + (normalizedPct - 0.2) * 1.0; } else { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 110)); intensity = 0.2 + normalizedPct * 0.5; } } else if (maxPct > 0.15) { const normalizedPct = percent / maxPct; const exponentialScale = Math.pow(normalizedPct, 0.7); if (exponentialScale > 0.7) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 10)); intensity = 0.7 + (exponentialScale - 0.7) * 0.8; } else if (exponentialScale > 0.4) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 40)); intensity = 0.45 + (exponentialScale - 0.4) * 0.8; } else { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 80)); intensity = 0.25 + exponentialScale * 0.5; } } const whiteRGB = [255, 255, 255]; const blendedRGB = interpolateColor(whiteRGB, targetRGB, intensity); const result = rgbToHex(...blendedRGB); colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function blendRelativePerformanceColor(baseColor, relativePercent, nationalAverage) { const cacheKey = `rel_perf_${baseColor}_${relativePercent.toFixed(4)}_${nationalAverage.toFixed(4)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; const baseRGB = hexToRgb(baseColor); let targetRGB; let intensity; let scalingFactor = 1.0; if (nationalAverage < 0.10) { scalingFactor = Math.max(0.1, nationalAverage * 10); } const performanceRatio = nationalAverage > 0 ? (relativePercent + nationalAverage) / nationalAverage : 1; if (nationalAverage < 0.10) { if (performanceRatio > 2.0) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.max(0, c - 30)); intensity = 0.95; } else if (performanceRatio > 1.5) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.max(0, c - 20)); intensity = 0.85; } else if (performanceRatio > 1.2) { targetRGB = baseRGB; intensity = 0.75; } else if (performanceRatio > 0.8) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 30)); intensity = 0.60; } else if (performanceRatio > 0.5) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 60)); intensity = 0.45; } else if (performanceRatio > 0.2) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 90)); intensity = 0.30; } else { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 120)); intensity = 0.20; } } else { if (relativePercent > 0.15) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.max(0, c - 30)); intensity = 0.95; } else if (relativePercent > 0.10) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.max(0, c - 20)); intensity = 0.90; } else if (relativePercent > 0.05) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.max(0, c - 10)); intensity = 0.85; } else if (relativePercent > 0.02) { targetRGB = baseRGB; intensity = 0.80; } else if (relativePercent > -0.02) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 20)); intensity = 0.65; } else if (relativePercent > -0.05) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 40)); intensity = 0.50; } else if (relativePercent > -0.10) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 70)); intensity = 0.35; } else if (relativePercent > -0.15) { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 100)); intensity = 0.25; } else { targetRGB = baseRGB.map(c => Math.min(255, c + 130)); intensity = 0.15; } } const whiteRGB = [255, 255, 255]; const blendedRGB = interpolateColor(whiteRGB, targetRGB, intensity); const result = rgbToHex(...blendedRGB); colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getCompetitivenessColor(margin) { const cacheKey = `comp_${margin.toFixed(4)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; let result; if (margin < 0.01) { result = "#FF0000"; } else if (margin < 0.025) { result = "#FF3300"; } else if (margin < 0.05) { result = "#FF6600"; } else if (margin < 0.075) { result = "#FF9900"; } else if (margin < 0.10) { result = "#FFCC00"; } else if (margin < 0.15) { result = "#FFFF00"; } else if (margin < 0.20) { result = "#AADD00"; } else if (margin < 0.30) { result = "#55BB00"; } else { result = "#0000FF"; } colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getTurnoutChangeColor(changePercent) { const cacheKey = `turnout_change_${changePercent.toFixed(2)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; let result; if (changePercent <= -10) { result = "#8B0000"; } else if (changePercent <= -7.5) { result = "#B22222"; } else if (changePercent <= -5) { result = "#FF0000"; } else if (changePercent <= -2.5) { result = "#FF6347"; } else if (changePercent <= -1) { result = "#FFA07A"; } else if (changePercent < 1) { result = "#F5F5F5"; } else if (changePercent < 2.5) { result = "#90EE90"; } else if (changePercent < 5) { result = "#3CB371"; } else if (changePercent < 7.5) { result = "#2E8B57"; } else if (changePercent < 10) { result = "#228B22"; } else { result = "#006400"; } colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getIssueStanceColor(stanceValue) { const cacheKey = `issue_stance_${stanceValue.toFixed(4)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; let stanceNumber = 7; if (campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json && campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json[0]) { const globalParams = campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json[0].fields; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_1_max) { stanceNumber = 1; } else if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_2_max) { stanceNumber = 2; } else if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_3_max) { stanceNumber = 3; } else if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_4_max) { stanceNumber = 4; } else if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_5_max) { stanceNumber = 5; } else if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_6_max) { stanceNumber = 6; } } else { if (stanceValue <= -0.71) { stanceNumber = 1; } else if (stanceValue <= -0.42) { stanceNumber = 2; } else if (stanceValue <= -0.13) { stanceNumber = 3; } else if (stanceValue <= 0.16) { stanceNumber = 4; } else if (stanceValue <= 0.45) { stanceNumber = 5; } else if (stanceValue <= 0.74) { stanceNumber = 6; } } const colors = [ "#8B0000", "#FF0000", "#FF8D1C", "#FFD700", "#32CD32", "#0000FF", "#000080" ]; const result = colors[stanceNumber - 1]; colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getStanceNumber(stanceValue) { if (campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json && campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json[0]) { const globalParams = campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json[0].fields; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_1_max) return 1; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_2_max) return 2; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_3_max) return 3; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_4_max) return 4; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_5_max) return 5; if (stanceValue <= globalParams.issue_stance_6_max) return 6; return 7; } else { if (stanceValue <= -0.71) return 1; if (stanceValue <= -0.42) return 2; if (stanceValue <= -0.13) return 3; if (stanceValue <= 0.16) return 4; if (stanceValue <= 0.45) return 5; if (stanceValue <= 0.74) return 6; return 7; } } function getIssueWeightColor(weight, issueIndex) { const cacheKey = `issue_weight_${weight.toFixed(4)}_${issueIndex}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; const weightRange = getIssueWeightRange(issueIndex); if (!weightRange || weightRange.min === weightRange.max || (weightRange.max - weightRange.min) <= 0.15) { return getStaticIssueWeightColor(weight); } const normalizedWeight = (weight - weightRange.min) / (weightRange.max - weightRange.min); const lightBlue = [248, 248, 255]; const darkBlue = [0, 0, 205]; const blendedRGB = interpolateColor(lightBlue, darkBlue, normalizedWeight); const result = rgbToHex(...blendedRGB); colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getIssueWeightRange(issueIndex) { if (!campaignTrail_temp.issues_json || issueIndex >= campaignTrail_temp.issues_json.length) { return null; } const issue = campaignTrail_temp.issues_json[issueIndex]; let minWeight = Infinity; let maxWeight = -Infinity; let hasData = false; campaignTrail_temp.final_state_results.forEach(stateResult => { const stateId = stateResult.state; const stateIssueData = campaignTrail_temp.state_issue_score_json?.find( sis => sis.fields.state === stateId && sis.fields.issue === issue.pk ); if (stateIssueData) { const weight = stateIssueData.fields.weight; minWeight = Math.min(minWeight, weight); maxWeight = Math.max(maxWeight, weight); hasData = true; } }); return hasData ? { min: minWeight, max: maxWeight } : null; } function getStaticIssueWeightColor(weight) { const normalizedWeight = Math.min(weight, 1.0); if (normalizedWeight < 0.1) { return "#F8F8FF"; } else if (normalizedWeight < 0.25) { return "#E6E6FA"; } else if (normalizedWeight < 0.4) { return "#B0C4DE"; } else if (normalizedWeight < 0.55) { return "#87CEEB"; } else if (normalizedWeight < 0.7) { return "#4682B4"; } else if (normalizedWeight < 0.85) { return "#1E90FF"; } else { return "#0000CD"; } } function getAverageIssueStanceColor(averageStance) { const cacheKey = `avg_issue_stance_${averageStance.toFixed(4)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; let stanceNumber = 7; if (campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json && campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json[0]) { const globalParams = campaignTrail_temp.global_parameter_json[0].fields; if (averageStance <= globalParams.issue_stance_1_max) { stanceNumber = 1; } else if (averageStance <= globalParams.issue_stance_2_max) { stanceNumber = 2; } else if (averageStance <= globalParams.issue_stance_3_max) { stanceNumber = 3; } else if (averageStance <= globalParams.issue_stance_4_max) { stanceNumber = 4; } else if (averageStance <= globalParams.issue_stance_5_max) { stanceNumber = 5; } else if (averageStance <= globalParams.issue_stance_6_max) { stanceNumber = 6; } } else { if (averageStance <= -0.71) { stanceNumber = 1; } else if (averageStance <= -0.42) { stanceNumber = 2; } else if (averageStance <= -0.13) { stanceNumber = 3; } else if (averageStance <= 0.16) { stanceNumber = 4; } else if (averageStance <= 0.45) { stanceNumber = 5; } else if (averageStance <= 0.74) { stanceNumber = 6; } } const colors = [ "#8B0000", "#FF0000", "#FF8D1C", "#FFD700", "#32CD32", "#0000FF", "#000080" ]; const result = colors[stanceNumber - 1]; colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getCandidateIssueAlignment(stateScore, candidateScore) { const cacheKey = `alignment_${stateScore.toFixed(4)}_${candidateScore.toFixed(4)}`; if (colorCache[cacheKey]) return colorCache[cacheKey]; const alignment = Math.abs(stateScore - candidateScore); let result; if (alignment < 0.2) { result = "#006400"; } else if (alignment < 0.4) { result = "#228B22"; } else if (alignment < 0.6) { result = "#32CD32"; } else if (alignment < 0.8) { result = "#FFD700"; } else if (alignment < 1.0) { result = "#FF8C00"; } else if (alignment < 1.2) { result = "#FF4500"; } else { result = "#8B0000"; } colorCache[cacheKey] = result; return result; } function getAverageCandidateIssueAlignment(stateAverageScore, candidateAverageScore) { const cacheKey = `avg_alignment_${stateAverageScore.toFixed(4)}_${candidateAverageScore.toFixed(4)}`;
